# 📍 Locality Guide: Ohio Pre-Statehood (Northwest Territory Era), c. 1790

---

## Jurisdictional Context & Boundary Changes

In 1790, what is now Ohio was entirely within the **Northwest Territory**, created by the Northwest Ordinance of 1787. This territory also encompassed present-day Indiana, Illinois, Michigan, Wisconsin, and part of Minnesota, governed from Marietta, OH. Ohio became the first state carved from it on **March 1, 1803**.

The county system was **fluid and rapidly changing** — the single most important research challenge for this period. Here is the succession that directly affects which records exist and where they are held:

| Date | Jurisdictional Event | Research Implication |
|---|---|---|
| Pre-1787 | British colonial / Virginia land claims | Any pre-settlement land claims follow Virginia or British records |
| **1787** | Northwest Ordinance creates the Northwest Territory | Records before this are colonial; territorial governance begins |
| **1788** | **Washington County** organized — 1st county, centered on Marietta | Nearly all settler records before 1790 file under Washington County |
| **1790** | **Hamilton County** organized — southwestern Ohio (Cincinnati area) | Settlers near present-day Cincinnati file under Hamilton County from this date |
| 1790s | Additional counties carved from Washington and Hamilton | A single farm may shift county jurisdiction mid-decade — always trace the parent county |
| **1803** | Ohio statehood | Record-keeping transitions from territorial to state administration |

> **Critical rule:** Records labeled "Northwest Territory" and records labeled "Washington County, Ohio" from 1788–1803 are one **continuous research trail** — not separate record sets. Work both together.

### Three Overlapping Land Survey Systems

Ohio is unusual in having **three distinct survey systems**, each with its own records repository — determining which district your ancestor lived in is essential before you can find their land records:

| District | Location | Survey Method | Primary Repository |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Virginia Military District** | Between the Scioto & Little Miami rivers (south-central OH) | Metes-and-bounds | **Virginia State Library, Richmond, VA** |
| **Connecticut Western Reserve** | Northeastern Ohio | Township grid | **Western Reserve Historical Society, Cleveland, OH** |
| **U.S. Military District / Seven Ranges** | Eastern & central Ohio | Federal rectangular survey | **BLM General Land Office** (glorecords.blm.gov) + NARA |

> ⚠️ **Virginia Military District researchers must go to Virginia first.** Bounty land warrants and grants for that district are not held in Ohio repositories at all — they are at the Virginia State Library in Richmond.

---

## Record Availability by Type

### 1. 🏆 Land Records — *Primary Source for 1790*
**Access: Indexed + Images (partial) | Some browse-only volumes**

- **FamilySearch Collection "Ohio, Land Records, 1787–1840"** (ID: 1542187) — 234,567 records, 117,283 images; dates back to 1787, fully overlapping the target period. Searchable by name.
- **Volume 005112233** — 51 images covering **Northwest Territory Territorial Land Records, 1788–1799**. ⚠️ **0% name-indexed, not full-text searchable — must be browsed image-by-image.**
- Federal land patents searchable at BLM GLO (glorecords.blm.gov) for public-domain tracts.

**Repositories:** Ohio History Connection (Columbus); BLM GLO website; Virginia State Library (VMD warrants).

---

### 2. 🏆 Church Records — *Often the Only Vital Records for 1790*
**Access: Indexed + Images**

Civil registration did not exist in 1790. Church registers are **frequently the sole surviving documentation** for births, marriages, and deaths in this era. Early settlements at Marietta and Cincinnati were served by Methodist, Presbyterian, and Moravian missionaries.

- **FamilySearch Collection "Ohio, Church Records, 1787–1950"** (ID: 2246917) — 678,234 records, 339,117 images; begins 1787.

**Repositories:** Denominational archives; Ohio History Connection microfilm holdings.

---

### 3. Marriage Records
**Access: Indexed + Images (sparse coverage for 1790)**

- **FamilySearch Collection "Ohio, County Marriages, 1789–2013"** (ID: 1803785) — 1,523,678 records, 761,839 images; begins 1789. Coverage of the 1789–1803 window exists but will be sparse — organized county court infrastructure was brand new.

> ⚠️ Before 1790, only Washington County existed to register marriages. If the relevant county wasn't yet organized at the time of an event, there may be no official record at all.

---

### 4. Tax Records
**Access: Indexed + Images (starts 1799–1800, not 1790)**

- **FamilySearch Collection "Ohio, Tax Records, 1800–1850"** (ID: 1987542) — 345,890 records, 172,945 images; starts 1800. Invaluable as a substitute: many settlers present in 1790 appear in the earliest tax lists.
- **Volume 005112240** — 73 images of **Washington County Tax Records, 1799–1803**. Only 12% name-indexed — the remaining 88% must be browsed image-by-image. Not full-text searchable.

> **Gap:** No digitized tax records for 1790–1799 appear in FamilySearch collections. Territorial-era tax volumes likely exist only on microfilm or in physical custody at the Ohio History Connection or county courthouses.

---

### 5. Census Records
**Access: National collection — Ohio not separately enumerated in 1790**

⚠️ Ohio was **not a state in 1790** and was not enumerated as a distinct unit in the 1790 federal census. The territory's population was tiny (mostly military and a handful of settlements at Marietta and Cincinnati).

- **First useful census: 1800 Federal Census** — the first enumerating what became Ohio. Settlers present in 1790 will likely appear here. Available on Ancestry.com and FamilySearch.
- **First Ohio-specific census: 1810** (as a state).

---

### 6. Military Records — *Revolutionary War & Frontier Service*
**Access: Physical + Microfilm; partially digitized at Fold3 and NARA**

Many 1790 Ohio settlers were Revolutionary War veterans who claimed bounty land in the territory:
- **NARA pension files and service records** — accessible via Fold3 (fold3.com) and archives.gov.
- **Virginia Military District bounty land warrants** — at the Virginia State Library (not Ohio).
- **Frontier wars of the 1790s** (Battle of Fallen Timbers, 1794; Treaty of Greenville, 1795) generated additional service records at NARA.

---

### 7. Probate & Court Records
**Access: Microfilm / Physical only — no indexed FamilySearch collections found**

Territorial-era probate and court records (1788–1803) were generated at the county level:
- **Washington County** (est. 1788): earliest records at Washington County Probate Court (Marietta) and microfilmed at the Ohio History Connection. Per the FamilySearch Wiki's Ohio Genealogy article, early court and land records are held at the **Ohio History Connection in Columbus**.
- **Hamilton County** (est. 1790): records at Hamilton County Probate Court (Cincinnati) and Ohio History Connection.

No FamilySearch-indexed collections for territorial-era probate appeared in the survey. Treat these as **microfilm or physical only** until confirmed otherwise.

---

### 8. Newspapers
**Access: Partially digitized**

The *Centinel of the North-Western Territory* (Cincinnati, 1793–1796) was Ohio's first newspaper and may contain land notices, deaths, and legal announcements. Access via **Newspapers.com** and the Ohio History Connection's digitization projects.

---

## Repositories at a Glance

| Repository | Key Holdings for c. 1790 |
|---|---|
| **Ohio History Connection**, Columbus, OH | Territorial/county microfilm, land, court, probate, tax records |
| **BLM General Land Office** (glorecords.blm.gov) | Federal land patents (public domain tracts) |
| **Virginia State Library**, Richmond, VA | Virginia Military District warrants and grants |
| **Western Reserve Historical Society**, Cleveland, OH | Connecticut Western Reserve land records |
| **NARA** (archives.gov / Fold3) | Military service records, pension files, bounty land warrants |
| **FamilySearch** | Collections 1542187, 2246917, 1803785; volumes 005112233, 005112240 |

---

## Gaps & Substitutes

| Gap | Best Substitute |
|---|---|
| No civil vital records before ~1803 | Church records (FS Collection 2246917, starts 1787) |
| Ohio not in the 1790 federal census | 1800 Federal Census; territorial-era tax lists |
| VMD land records not in Ohio repositories | Virginia State Library, Richmond, VA |
| Territorial land records largely unindexed | Browse image group 005112233 (51 images, NW Territory Land Records 1788–1799) manually |
| Sparse tax records before 1800 | Image group 005112240 (Washington Co. tax records, 1799–1803, 12% indexed) |
| No statewide death registration until 1867 | Church burials; probate inventories; newspaper death notices |

---

## Quick-Reference Research Rules for 1790 Ohio

1. **County first, always.** In 1790 only Washington County (1788) and Hamilton County (1790) existed. Identify which county held your ancestor's township before searching.
2. **"Northwest Territory" = "Washington County" records are one trail.** Territorial and early county records are a single continuous research thread — search both together under the same person.
3. **Virginia Military District researchers: start in Virginia.** Bounty land warrants for that district are at the Virginia State Library, not in Ohio.
4. **Church records are your vital records.** The FS "Ohio, Church Records, 1787–1950" collection is often the only source for 1790-era births, marriages, and deaths.
5. **Most territorial volumes require manual browsing.** Image group 005112233 (NW Territory Land Records, 1788–1799) is **0% indexed** — plan to scroll all 51 images by hand.
6. **The 1800 census is your census.** Skip 1790 for Ohio; head straight to 1800 as your first enumeration source.

# 📍 California, United States — 1850s Gold Rush Research Guide

**Place:** California, United States | **Period:** 1848–1865 (Gold Rush era, statehood 1850–)
**Focus:** Mining and settlement migration

---

## Jurisdictional Context

California was Mexican territory until ceded to the United States by the Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo (February 1848) — the same year gold was discovered at Sutter's Mill. Crucially, **California bypassed territorial status entirely and was admitted as a state in 1850**, meaning civil infrastructure (county courts, recorders, vital registration) had to be built from scratch almost immediately. The original 27 counties were created in 1850; more were carved out through the decade as population spread into the Sierra Nevada foothills. The Gold Rush (1848–1855) brought an estimated **300,000 people** from across the United States and around the world.

Record quality in the 1850s is consequently uneven: institutions were brand new, registration compliance was patchy, and a highly transient, predominantly male population was difficult to capture in any single record set. Triangulating across multiple record classes is essential.

---

## Record Classes: What Exists, Where, and How to Access It

### 1. 🗂️ Census Records — **Start Here**

**1850 U.S. Federal Census** *(first federal census taken in California)*
The essential anchor for anyone present at statehood. **Caution: this census was partially destroyed — some California county returns are missing** (per the FamilySearch Wiki's *California Genealogy* article). Indexed and available via FamilySearch, Ancestry, and Fold3. Check which counties survive before assuming absence.

**1852 California State Census** *(unique Gold Rush-era resource)*
FamilySearch holds a digitized volume (imageGroupNumber `006601001`, 522 images) covering 1850–1852.
- Access level: **Indexed + images — 88% name-indexed and full-text searchable** — excellent access.
- This is the only surviving California state census from the era and captures the peak Gold Rush wave. It is the primary substitute for missing 1850 federal county returns and the only census source between 1850 and 1860.

**1860 U.S. Federal Census** — Fully survived for California. Use in tandem with the 1852 state census to track who stayed and who left.

**Federal Mortality Schedules (1850, 1860)** — Record deaths in the year preceding each enumeration. Critical substitute for absent death registration in the 1850s.

---

### 2. ⛏️ Mining Records — Browse-Only; Mostly Physical

**Mining Claim Registers (San Francisco area, 1849–1855)**
FamilySearch holds a digitized volume (imageGroupNumber `006601002`, 340 images).
- Access level: **Browse-only — 0% name-indexed, NOT full-text searchable.** Must be reviewed image by image; no keyword or name search possible.

Per the FamilySearch Wiki (*California Genealogy*, "Gold Rush Records"), mining claim records across the state were filed at **county recorders' offices** — and for most counties, they exist **only as physical records** at the courthouse or county archives. There is no statewide digitized mining claim index. Identify the specific county of interest (El Dorado, Placer, Nevada, Tuolumne, Calaveras, and Amador were the main mining counties) and contact the county recorder or historical society directly.

---

### 3. 🚢 Arrival & Immigration Records

**Ship Passenger Manifests (San Francisco arrivals)**
The great majority of sea-route migrants arrived through San Francisco. These manifests are held at the **National Archives, Pacific Region, San Bruno, California**, and are also available via Ancestry.com and Fold3.com. The FamilySearch Wiki (*California Genealogy*) explicitly identifies ship passenger manifests as a key Gold Rush source.

**Port Arrival Registers (Sacramento, 1849–1858)**
FamilySearch holds a digitized volume (imageGroupNumber `006601003`, 298 images).
- Access level: **Browse-only — 0% name-indexed, NOT full-text searchable.** Must be browsed image by image.

**Overland Trail Migrants**
No single record set captures those who came by land. Consult:
- Trail diaries and journals at the **Bancroft Library (UC Berkeley)** and **California State Library (Sacramento)** — both hold extensive manuscript collections on overland migrants (per the FamilySearch Wiki).
- Overlanders frequently appear in county deed, voter, and court records upon arrival.

---

### 4. 📋 Vital Records (Civil Registration)

California had no statewide vital registration infrastructure in the 1850s. County-level registration began unevenly:

| Collection | FamilySearch ID | Records | Images | Date Range | Access |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| California, County Births | 2017276 | 789,456 | 394,728 | 1850–1919 | Indexed + images |
| California, County Marriages | 1452832 | 567,890 | 283,945 | 1850–1952 | Indexed + images |

⚠️ **Key caveat:** Civil registration in the 1850s was sparse and inconsistently followed — county compliance varied widely. Absence of a county birth or death record for the 1850s is common and does not prove the event did not occur. Use church registers, mortality schedules, newspapers, and cemetery records as substitutes.

**No indexed California death collection exists for the 1850s** — statewide death registration did not begin until 1905. For 1850s deaths, use mortality schedules, cemetery transcriptions (Find A Grave), and newspapers.

---

### 5. 🗳️ Great Registers (Voter Registration) — Powerful, But Starts 1866

**California, Great Registers, 1866–1910** (FamilySearch collection 1923340)
- 1,345,678 indexed records | 672,839 images
- Access level: **Indexed + images**

These begin in **1866**, outside the core 1850s Gold Rush decade — but they are among the most genealogically rich California sources because each entry records the voter's **full name, age/birth year, birthplace/nativity, occupation, and physical description.** For any adult male who arrived during the Gold Rush and remained in California, the Great Registers are the best tool for confirming identity and origin. Plan to use them as a follow-on step after establishing 1850s presence.

---

### 6. 🏡 Land Records

California is a **federal public-domain state** — the US government held and distributed land after 1848.

- **Federal land patents** (BLM General Land Office): searchable free at [glorecords.blm.gov](https://glorecords.blm.gov). Essential for miners and settlers who received land grants.
- **County deed records** begin 1850 at county courthouses. The California State Archives (Sacramento) holds copies for many counties.
- The FamilySearch Wiki notes land grants, deeds, and patents are key sources for documenting settlement and migration.

---

### 7. ⚖️ Court, Naturalization & Probate Records

Pre-1906 naturalizations could be filed in **any court of record** — county, state, or federal. For a Gold Rush population that was heavily multinational (Chinese, Chilean, Mexican, German, Irish, and other European miners), naturalization petitions are especially valuable. They typically state birthplace, arrival date, and renounced allegiance.

- County court records from the 1850s are held at **county courthouses** or the **California State Archives**.
- Probate files (wills, estate inventories) document family structure and assets — useful even for miners who died without reaching old age.
- Court records may also include guardianship files and civil suits naming relatives.

---

### 8. ⛪ Church Records

**California, Mission Records, 1769–1850** (FamilySearch collection 1804372, 98,765 records) — these predate the Gold Rush and document Native Californians and Mexican-era settlers; useful as background context for pre-statehood families, not Gold Rush migrants.

Gold Rush-era church records (Protestant denominations and Catholic parishes) are held at **denominational archives** and **local historical societies**. No statewide digitized index exists. Identify the denomination and nearest church for the mining camp or town of interest.

---

### 9. 📰 Newspapers

**Newspapers.com** is among the FamilySearch-curated external links for California. Gold Rush California had active newspapers — the *Alta California* (San Francisco) was the most prominent — carrying:
- Ship arrival lists
- Death and obituary notices
- Mining claim legal notices
- Business and merchant advertisements

Newspapers are especially productive for merchants, prominent miners, and public figures; also useful for tracking transient individuals who appear nowhere else.

---

### 10. 🎖️ Military Records

**Fold3.com** is among the FamilySearch-curated external links. Veterans of the **Mexican-American War (1846–1848)** who subsequently settled in California during the Gold Rush may have pension and service records at NARA. Some Gold Rush-era California residents also served in early California militia units; state military records are at the California State Archives.

---

### 11. 🪦 Cemetery Records

**Find A Grave** (findagrave.com) is among the FamilySearch-curated external links. With no statewide death registration until 1905, cemetery transcriptions and tombstone photographs are often the only death documentation available for the 1850s. Many Gold Rush-era mining camps had improvised cemeteries, some of which have been documented by local historical societies.

---

## Repository Quick-Reference

| Repository | Holdings Relevant to 1850s | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch** (familysearch.org) | 1852 state census, county births/marriages, mining claim volumes, port arrivals, Great Registers (1866+) | Free; browse-only for mining/port volumes |
| **National Archives, Pacific Region — San Bruno, CA** | Ship passenger manifests (SF arrivals), federal court records, naturalization files | Physical + some on Ancestry/Fold3 |
| **California State Archives, Sacramento** | County court records, naturalization files, state government records | Physical; some digitized |
| **California State Library, Sacramento** | Manuscripts, Gold Rush diaries, newspapers, maps | Physical; some digitized |
| **Bancroft Library, UC Berkeley** | Extensive Gold Rush manuscript collections, overland journals, oral histories | Physical; some digitized |
| **County Courthouses / Recorders** | Mining claim registers, deeds, marriages, probate, court records | Mostly physical — contact individually |
| **BLM General Land Office** (glorecords.blm.gov) | Federal land patents | Free online |
| **Ancestry.com** | 1850/1860 census, passenger lists, newspapers, Fold3 military | Subscription |
| **Fold3.com** | Military service, pension, and Civil War records | Subscription |
| **Newspapers.com** | California historical newspapers | Subscription |
| **Find A Grave** (findagrave.com) | Cemetery transcriptions statewide | Free |

---

## Digitization Access Summary

| Record Type | Years | FamilySearch Access Level |
|---|---|---|
| California State Census | 1850–1852 | ✅ Indexed + images (88%; full-text searchable) |
| County Births | 1850–1919 | ✅ Indexed + images |
| County Marriages | 1850–1952 | ✅ Indexed + images |
| Great Registers | 1866–1910 | ✅ Indexed + images *(starts after 1850s)* |
| Mission Records | 1769–1850 | ✅ Indexed + images *(pre-Gold Rush)* |
| Mining Claim Registers | 1849–1855 | ⚠️ Browse-only (0% indexed; no full-text) |
| Port Arrival Registers | 1849–1858 | ⚠️ Browse-only (0% indexed; no full-text) |
| Ship Passenger Manifests | 1849–1860s | Physical (NARA San Bruno) + Ancestry/Fold3 |
| Federal Census (1850, 1860) | 1850, 1860 | ✅ Indexed *(1850 partially destroyed)* |
| Mortality Schedules | 1850, 1860 | ✅ Indexed |
| Federal Land Patents | 1850s+ | ✅ Free online (BLM GLO) |

---

## ⚠️ Critical Quirks for Gold Rush Research

1. **The 1850 federal census is partially destroyed** — some California county returns are missing. Always use the **1852 state census** as the primary substitute and check before assuming a person wasn't enumerated.
2. **Mining claim registers on FamilySearch are browse-only** (0% indexed, no full-text search). For most counties, mining records survive **only in physical repositories** at the county recorder's office — contact the county directly.
3. **No statewide death registration until 1905.** For 1850s deaths, use the 1850 and 1860 mortality schedules, Find A Grave, and newspaper death notices.
4. **Civil birth registration was sparse and unevenly enforced** in the 1850s — absence of a record is common and not proof of non-occurrence.
5. **Great Registers don't begin until 1866** — plan them as a follow-on, not a primary, Gold Rush-decade source.
6. **The Gold Rush population was heavily multinational.** Chinese, Chilean, Mexican, German, Irish, and other European miners are documented; consider searching origin-country records in parallel. Naturalization files are especially productive for identifying birthplace and arrival date.
7. **FamilySearch's volume_search returned 14 total volumes** for this period; only 3 were shown on the first page. Additional volumes may cover other California counties or record types — search directly on FamilySearch for your specific county of interest.
8. **County boundaries shifted rapidly in the 1850s** — a mining camp filed in one county in 1850 may have been transferred to a newly carved county by 1855. Always verify county jurisdiction for the specific year and location of interest.

---

*Sources: FamilySearch collections_search and volume_search results for California, United States (1845–1870); FamilySearch Wiki, "California Genealogy" (Gold Rush Records and Pre-Statehood Records sections); Wikipedia, "California Gold Rush"; FamilySearch-curated external links for California.*

I'll survey the records and repositories for New York City in the colonial 1700s. Let me start by resolving the jurisdiction.Good. Standard place is **"New York, New York, United States"** (city, founded 1624). Now I'll fire all survey calls in a single parallel batch.The wiki search surfaced a direct NYC genealogy article. Let me read it now, and also run a broader volume search at the state level to catch additional digitized volumes.I now have all the survey data I need. Let me compile and present the complete locality guide.

---

# 🗽 Locality Research Guide
## New York City — Colonial Period (1700s)
*Jurisdiction: New York County / Province of New York · Survey date: 21 July 2026*

---

## Jurisdictional Context

New York City's colonial genealogy spans two distinct imperial administrations that directly affect which records were kept, in what language, and by whom:

- **Dutch period (1626–1664):** Founded as *New Amsterdam* under the Dutch West India Company. Records were kept in **Dutch**, and the dominant church was the Dutch Reformed (Collegiate) Church.
- **British period (1664–1783):** Renamed New York upon English seizure. Records shifted to English; the Church of England (Anglican) became prominent alongside continuing Dutch Reformed, Presbyterian, Quaker, Jewish, and Lutheran communities.

Throughout the 1700s, New York City was the seat of **New York County** and the capital of the **Province of New York**. Provincial administration — courts, land patents, probate — was centralized here, which means NYC is often the place of record even for families living elsewhere in the province.

> ⚠️ **Critical gap:** New York State did not begin **civil registration** of births, marriages, and deaths until **1847**, and NYC registration was inconsistently enforced well into the late 19th century. There are **no civil vital records** for the colonial 1700s. Church registers and church-affiliated records are the primary — often the only — substitute.

---

## Record Types, Availability & Access

### 1. Church Records ⭐ *Primary source for vital events*

**What they contain:** Baptisms, marriages, and burials — the colonial-era equivalent of civil vital records.

**Key denominations in colonial NYC:**
| Congregation | Language | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Dutch Reformed (Collegiate) Church | Dutch → English | Oldest continuous registers; baptisms from 1639 |
| Trinity Church (Anglican/Episcopal) | English | Major 18th-c. congregation; burials and baptisms |
| First Presbyterian Church | English | Founded 1716 |
| Shearith Israel (Spanish & Portuguese Synagogue) | Hebrew/Portuguese | Oldest US Jewish congregation (1654) |
| Lutheran, Quaker, French Huguenot | Various | Smaller but extant registers |

**On FamilySearch:**
- **Collection: [New York, Church Records, 1640–1940](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1469062)** — name-indexed with images; 2.3 million records spanning the colonial period. Search here first.
- **Digitized volume 007733180:** Dutch Reformed Church Registers, New York City, **1700–1776** — 211 images, **0% name-indexed, not full-text searchable, in Dutch**. Must be browsed image-by-image and read in the original Dutch language.

**Other repositories:**
- **New-York Historical Society (N-YHS):** Holds original registers and transcripts for many denominations.
- **NY Genealogical & Biographical Society (NYG&B):** Has published transcriptions and indexes of colonial church registers, some digitized at [newyorkfamilyhistory.org](https://www.newyorkfamilyhistory.org).

---

### 2. Land Records

**What they contain:** Deeds, patents, grants, conveyances, and mortgages recording property ownership and transfer.

**On FamilySearch:**
- **Collection: [New York, Land Records, 1630–1975](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/2078654)** — name-indexed with images; 1.5 million records. Covers colonial-era conveyances and grants.

**Other repositories:**
- **New York State Archives (Albany):** Original colonial land patents and grants issued by the Provincial government. The *New York Colonial Patents* series begins in the 17th century.
- **NYC Municipal Archives:** Original deed books for New York County.

> **Key strategy:** Land records are the backbone of colonial NYC research — they identify neighbors, family clusters, and economic status, and they survive far better than any other record class for this period.

---

### 3. Probate Records ⭐

**What they contain:** Wills, estate inventories, letters of administration, and guardianship records. Wills in colonial New York were proved before the **Prerogative Court of the Colony of New York** — centralized in NYC.

**On FamilySearch:**
- **Collection: [New York, Probate Records, 1629–1971](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1542780)** — name-indexed with images; nearly 1 million records. Colonial-era wills are within scope.

**Published finding aids:**
- **Abstracts of Wills on File in the Surrogate's Office, City of New York, 1665–1800** (17 vols.) — published by the New-York Historical Society; widely available in libraries and partially digitized. This is an essential index for colonial NYC wills.

**Other repositories:**
- **New York Surrogate's Court (NYC):** Original will files and administration records.
- **NY State Archives:** Colonial Prerogative Court records.

---

### 4. Tax and Assessment Records

**What they contain:** Ward-level lists of taxable residents and their assessed property — function as a census substitute identifying heads of household by year and neighborhood ward.

**On FamilySearch:**
- **Collection: [New York, Tax Assessment Rolls of Real and Personal Estates, 1799–1804](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1678432)** — 123,000 records; overlaps only the very tail of the colonial period (1799–1800 if your window extends to 1800). Useful for transition-era research.

**For the pre-1799 period:**
- Colonial-era tax lists survive for scattered years and are held at the **NY State Archives** and **NYC Municipal Archives**. The FamilySearch wiki notes that ward-level tax assessments are important substitutes for census-type records.

---

### 5. Colonial Provincial & Court Records

**What they contain:** The **Mayor's Court** (NYC's primary civil court) and the **Court of Chancery** generated case files naming litigants, witnesses, and their relationships. Colonial manuscripts also include council minutes, governor's papers, and naturalization oaths.

**Repositories:**
- **New York State Archives (Albany):** *New York Colonial Manuscripts* — the core archive of provincial government records through 1783. Accessible in person and partially microfilmed.
- **NYC Municipal Archives:** Mayor's Court records and city administrative records.
- **Library of Congress / NARA:** Some provincial records in colonial-era federal precursors.

---

### 6. Militia Records

**What they contain:** Lists of men of military age by ward and county — useful for identifying male residents, ages, and neighborhoods when no other record survives.

- Colonial New York Province militia rolls are noted in the FamilySearch wiki as surviving for some years; held primarily at the **NY State Archives**.
- **Fold3** (military records) and **NARA** hold some colonial-era militia and muster records.

---

### 7. Immigration and Passenger Records

**What they contain:** New York City was the principal port of entry for the colonial period. Passenger lists survive unevenly; the pre-1820 period has patchy federal records.

- **NARA** holds some pre-1820 customs passenger lists.
- The **NY Genealogical & Biographical Society** has published colonial-era arrival records and naturalization lists.
- Naturalization oaths were administered in multiple courts — not centralized until 1906.

---

### 8. Newspapers

**What they contain:** Birth, marriage, and death announcements; runaway servant notices; land sales; and legal notices naming individuals.

- **New York Gazette** (founded 1725) is the key colonial newspaper.
- **Accessible Archive** and **Readex/Newsbank** hold digitized colonial newspapers.
- **Newspapers.com** and **Chronicling America** have limited colonial coverage for NYC.

---

### 9. Census Records

The first federal census is **1790** — post-colonial, but it captures families who lived through the 1700s and is the starting point for working backward. No federal or colonial census covers the 1700s as such; tax lists and militia rolls are the primary substitutes.

---

## Key Repositories at a Glance

| Repository | What's Held | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch.org** | Digitized church, land, probate collections | Free online |
| **NY State Archives (Albany)** | Colonial Manuscripts, militia, land patents | In-person; some microfilm |
| **NYC Municipal Archives** | Deed books, Mayor's Court, city records | In-person; partial online |
| **New-York Historical Society** | Church registers, Abstracts of Wills, manuscripts | In-person; some digitized |
| **NY Genealogical & Biographical Society** | Published indexes, church transcriptions | [newyorkfamilyhistory.org](https://www.newyorkfamilyhistory.org) (subscription) |
| **NARA (Washington & NYC)** | Colonial-era federal precursors, military | In-person; Fold3 online |
| **Fold3.com** | Colonial militia, military records | Subscription |

---

## Key Research Quirks & Warnings

1. **No civil vital records for the 1700s.** Civil registration began in New York in 1847. Church registers are the only vital-record substitute — identify your ancestor's denomination before searching.
2. **Dutch Reformed registers are in Dutch and browse-only on FamilySearch.** Volume 007733180 (211 images, 1700–1776) is 0% name-indexed and not full-text searchable. You must browse image-by-image and read 18th-century Dutch handwriting.
3. **Probate was provincially centralized.** All colonial wills were proved in NYC at the Prerogative Court regardless of where the testator lived — so NYC probate records cover the whole province, not just city residents.
4. **The 1790 census is your earliest federal starting point.** Work backward from 1790 using church records, land deeds, and probate to push into the colonial period.
5. **Ward geography matters.** NYC was divided into wards (East Ward, West Ward, Dock Ward, etc.) that appear on tax lists and court records. Knowing your ancestor's ward narrows searches significantly.
6. **NYG&B published transcriptions are essential.** The *New York Genealogical and Biographical Record* (available at the NYG&B and many research libraries) contains colonial-era church transcriptions not yet on FamilySearch.
7. **The FamilySearch wiki place page for New York returned only generic content** during this survey — consult the [New York Genealogy wiki](https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/New_York_Genealogy) and [New York City Genealogy wiki](https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/New_York_City_Genealogy) pages directly for jurisdiction-specific guidance.

---

## Digitization Summary

| Record Type | FamilySearch Availability | Access Level |
|---|---|---|
| Church records (general) | Collection 1469062, 1640–1940 | ✅ Indexed + images |
| Dutch Reformed registers (city, 1700–1776) | Volume 007733180, 211 images | ⚠️ Browse-only · Dutch language |
| Land records | Collection 2078654, 1630–1975 | ✅ Indexed + images |
| Probate records | Collection 1542780, 1629–1971 | ✅ Indexed + images |
| Tax rolls (1799–1804 only) | Collection 1678432 | ✅ Indexed + images |
| Colonial manuscripts / militia | NY State Archives | 🏛️ Physical / microfilm only |
| Mayor's Court records | NYC Municipal Archives | 🏛️ Physical only |
| Pre-1799 tax lists | NY State Archives, NYC Municipal Archives | 🏛️ Physical / microfilm only |

---

**Bottom line for colonial NYC research:** Church records on FamilySearch (collection 1469062) and the digitized probate (1542780) and land (2078654) collections are your most searchable starting points. For the Dutch Reformed congregation specifically, expect browse-only Dutch-language registers. Beyond FamilySearch, the NYG&B published record series and the NY State Archives' Colonial Manuscripts are indispensable — much of the most important colonial material has not yet been digitized or indexed online.

---

# Locality Research Guide
## Virginia — American Revolutionary War Era, 1775–1783
### Focus: Military Service & Wartime Activities

---

## Jurisdictional Context

Virginia was one of the thirteen original colonies and became a U.S. state in 1788. At the outbreak of the Revolution the territory functioned as a royal colony under county-court governance — a system already well established since the 1630s. County courts recorded deeds, wills, estate inventories, tax rolls, and militia musters, making Virginia one of the best-documented jurisdictions for the Revolutionary period.

**Critical boundary note:** Virginia counties were frequently subdivided throughout the 18th century. You must identify your ancestor's county of residence for the *specific years* 1775–1783, not simply "Virginia," to locate the right court-level records. Use the [Virginia County Formation page](https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Virginia_County_Formation) to trace which parent county held jurisdiction before a new county was created.

**Critical gap — the missing 1790 census:** Virginia's 1790 federal census was destroyed when the British burned Washington in 1814. County personal property tax lists (beginning 1782) and land tax lists are the universally accepted substitutes and are indispensable for placing a family in a specific county at the close of the war.

---

## Record Classes & Repositories

### 🏅 1. Continental Army Service Records & Muster Rolls
- **What they are:** Compiled Military Service Records (CMSRs) document a soldier's name, rank, unit, dates of service, and pay. Muster rolls list men present in a unit at a specific date.
- **Repository:** National Archives and Records Administration (NARA), Washington D.C. — **Record Group 93**
- **Access:** Fold3.com has digitized and indexed a large portion of Revolutionary War CMSRs. NARA's online catalog (catalog.archives.gov) also holds scanned images. Physical originals at NARA Archives I, Washington D.C.
- **FamilySearch collection:** *Virginia, Military Records, 1775–1783* (collection ID 1945678) — verify coverage and searchability directly on FamilySearch.
- **Source on FamilySearch Wiki:** [Virginia Military Records — Revolutionary War](https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Virginia_Military_Records#Revolutionary_War)

### 🏅 2. Virginia Militia Records
- **What they are:** Muster rolls, pay vouchers, and officer commissions for state militia units — entirely separate from the Continental Line. Virginia's militia was enormous; many men served only short local tours and appear only here, not in federal records.
- **Repository:** **Library of Virginia (LVA)**, Richmond — the primary custodian of Virginia state-level military records. The LVA's online catalog (lva.virginia.gov) is searchable.
- **Access:** Partial digitization via FamilySearch microfilm. Many volumes remain browse-only or in physical form at the LVA. Check the LVA's *Guide to Virginia Military Organizations, 1774–1865* for unit-level guidance.
- **Do not conflate** Continental Line service (federal, NARA RG 93) with militia service (state, Library of Virginia) — a man could appear in both or in only one.

### 🏅 3. Pension & Bounty-Land Warrant Application Files
- **What they are:** Veterans (and their widows/heirs) filed pension applications under acts of 1818, 1832, and later years. These files often contain biographical narratives — birth date and place, unit served, battles fought, marriage date, names of children — making them among the most genealogically rich records of the era. Bounty-land warrant applications (for 160-acre grants) are in the same RG.
- **Repository:** NARA — **Record Group 15**
- **Access:** Fold3.com has fully digitized and indexed the *Revolutionary War Pension and Bounty-Land Warrant Application Files*. Ancestry.com also hosts this collection. Many are free on [Fold3 and FamilySearch](https://www.fold3.com/). The published *Index of Revolutionary War Pension Applications* (Pension Index) by the National Genealogical Society is a standard finding aid.
- **Note:** A pension file may be the *only* surviving document proving service for a militia soldier whose muster roll did not survive.

### 🏅 4. Virginia State Navy Records
- **What they are:** Virginia maintained its own state navy during the Revolution. Service records, pay lists, and muster rolls for the Virginia State Navy are separate from Continental Navy records.
- **Repository:** Library of Virginia; also cited in *Virginia Military Records* on the FamilySearch Wiki. Some records published in the *Calendar of Virginia State Papers*.
- **Access:** Partially microfilmed; check LVA catalog and FamilySearch for digitized volumes.

### 🏅 5. Virginia Military District of Ohio — Bounty Land Warrants
- **What they are:** Virginia reserved a tract in present-day Ohio (the Virginia Military District) to fulfill its land-bounty promises to veterans. Warrant and survey records connect veterans to specific grants.
- **Repository:** Library of Virginia (warrant records); Ohio History Connection (survey records and final patents); Bureau of Land Management General Land Office (glorecords.blm.gov) for patent images.
- **Research tip from the FamilySearch Wiki ([Virginia Military Records — Post-War Bounty Lands](https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Virginia_Military_Records#Post-War_Bounty_Lands)):** Even if your ancestor never relocated to Ohio, he may still have filed a warrant — or assigned it to a land speculator — leaving a paper trail in both Virginia and Ohio repositories.

---

### 📋 6. County Personal Property & Land Tax Lists, 1782–1850
- **What they are:** Annual county-level assessments listing taxable white males (and enslaved persons, livestock, carriages, etc.). The 1782 personal property tax — the first statewide enumeration — is the closest proxy to a 1780 census and was taken just as the war ended.
- **FamilySearch collection:** *Virginia, Tax Records, 1782–1850* (ID 1456723)
- **Why they matter for military research:** These lists name men of fighting age in each county at the war's close, document household size, and can confirm a veteran was present in a given county. The 1782 lists are especially valuable as a post-war "roll call."
- **Access:** Largely indexed on FamilySearch; also on Ancestry and HeritageQuest through many public libraries.

### 📋 7. County Court Records (Deeds, Wills, Estates, Bonds)
- **FamilySearch collection:** *Virginia, County Court Records, 1650–1920* (ID 2134567)
- **FamilySearch collection:** *Virginia, Land, Marriage, and Will Records, 1600–1900* (ID 1734285)
- **What to look for:**
  - **Wills and estate inventories (1775–1790):** Name heirs and creditors; a soldier's estate settled during or after the war may list military equipment or outstanding pay.
  - **Guardian bonds:** If a soldier died leaving minor children, a court-appointed guardian generated records naming the children and their father.
  - **Deed books:** Land purchases just after the war often reflect bounty-land proceeds or enlistment bonuses converted to real property.
  - **Jury lists and processioners' records:** Confirm a man was present in the county and considered a free white householder.
- **Repository:** County courthouses (originals); LVA (many microfilmed); FamilySearch (digitized)

### ⛪ 8. Church Records
- **FamilySearch collection:** *Virginia, Church Records, 1680–1929* (ID 1598230)
- **Context:** The Church of England (Anglican) was the established church in colonial Virginia. After the Revolution it reorganized as the Protestant Episcopal Church. Vestry books recorded births/christenings, marriages, and burials by parish.
- **Why they matter for military research:** A soldier baptized in the 1740s–1760s can be found here; a soldier's children born during the war may appear in vestry registers. Marriage bonds and banns filed at the county court supplement church registers.
- **Repository:** Many original vestry books at LVA; Episcopal Diocese archives; some at local historical societies. FamilySearch holds microfilm for many parishes. The published *Virginia Colonial Records Project* preserves transcript data for heavily damaged originals.
- **Note:** Not all parishes survived disestablishment. If a vestry book is missing, check county court marriage bonds and neighboring parish registers.

### 📰 9. Historical Newspapers
- **What they are:** Virginia had several newspapers during the war, including the *Virginia Gazette* (Williamsburg). These published muster calls, casualty notices, Loyalist land confiscation orders, and advertisements by or for soldiers.
- **Access:** Newspapers.com; Chronicling America (chroniclingamerica.loc.gov — free); Colonial Williamsburg Foundation digital archives.
- **Research tip:** Confiscation acts against Loyalists were published in the *Virginia Gazette* and are useful for both Patriot and Loyalist family research.

### 🏛️ 10. Loyalist Claims & Records
- **What they are:** Virginians who remained loyal to the Crown filed compensation claims with the British government after the war. Files may include depositions naming family members, neighbors, and property.
- **Repository:** UK National Archives, Kew — *Audit Office 12 and 13 series* (AO 12, AO 13). Available on microfilm and partially digitized through Ancestry (search "American Loyalist Claims").
- **Companion source:** Virginia's Sequestration Acts (1779) and Confiscation Acts (1779–1782) are recorded in Virginia legislative papers at LVA, listing Loyalists whose property was seized. These can document family and property connections from the Patriot side of the record.

---

## Repository Summary

| Repository | Holdings Relevant to 1775–1783 | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **NARA, Washington D.C.** | Continental Line CMSRs (RG 93); pension/bounty-land files (RG 15) | Fold3, Ancestry, in-person |
| **Library of Virginia (LVA), Richmond** | Militia records, state navy, tax lists, county court records, land grants | lva.virginia.gov; FamilySearch microfilm |
| **FamilySearch.org** | 5 Virginia collections overlapping 1775–1783 (see above IDs) | Free online |
| **Fold3.com** | Digitized/indexed CMSRs, pension files, Virginia-specific military | Subscription |
| **Ancestry.com** | Revolutionary War pensions, tax lists, newspapers | Subscription |
| **National Archives UK (Kew)** | Loyalist claims (AO 12, AO 13) | In-person; Ancestry microfilm |
| **FindAGrave.com** | Cemetery records — may document veterans' burial dates and units | Free online |
| **BLM GLO (glorecords.blm.gov)** | Virginia Military District of Ohio patents | Free online |

---

## Key Research Quirks & Gotchas

1. **No 1790 Virginia census exists.** Use 1782–1800 county personal property tax lists (FamilySearch collection 1456723) as direct substitutes. They are the foundational tool for placing a Revolutionary War family.
2. **Continental Line ≠ Militia.** Records for each are at *different repositories* (NARA vs. Library of Virginia). Search both independently — a man could appear in neither if records were lost.
3. **Pension files are your richest source.** Even a brief or disputed application contains biographical data found nowhere else. Search Fold3 first; if no pension was filed, check whether a widow or child applied under later acts.
4. **Virginia counties were constantly subdividing.** Identify the county your ancestor lived in *during* 1775–1783, not after. A man whose descendants lived in post-1800 counties may have been recorded in a parent county that no longer exists.
5. **Virginia Military District bounty lands are in Ohio, but the warrants are in Virginia.** Always check both repositories even if your ancestor never left Virginia.
6. **Church records have gaps post-disestablishment.** The Anglican vestry system collapsed after 1776; records from 1776–c.1800 are patchier than colonial-era registers. County court marriage bonds and Methodist/Baptist registers fill many gaps.
7. **The FamilySearch Wiki article [Virginia Military Records](https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Virginia_Military_Records)** is the most detailed single source on this record class and should be read in full before planning searches. The sections on the Revolutionary War and Post-War Bounty Lands are especially relevant.

---

## Suggested Search Sequence

1. **Start with the pension index** (Fold3 or Ancestry) — it either produces a rich file or quickly rules out a federal pension.
2. **Search NARA RG 93 CMSRs** (Fold3) for Continental Line service.
3. **Check Library of Virginia militia records** for state-level service not captured at NARA.
4. **Pull 1782 personal property tax lists** (FamilySearch, collection 1456723) to place the family geographically at war's end.
5. **Search county court deed and will books** (collections 1734285 and 2134567) for estate or land transactions 1775–1790.
6. **Check Virginia Military District of Ohio** warrant records at LVA and BLM GLO for a bounty-land trail.
7. **Search church records** (collection 1598230) for the pre-war baptism of the soldier and wartime events for his family.
8. **For suspected Loyalist families**, search UK National Archives AO 12/AO 13 and Virginia's Sequestration/Confiscation Act records at LVA.

---

# 📍 New Orleans, Orleans Parish, Louisiana — Early 1800s Locality Research Guide

**Jurisdiction:** New Orleans, Orleans, Louisiana, United States (city established 1718)
**Target period:** c. 1800–1850
**Research question focus:** What records survived disasters, where copies or substitutes are held, and how to trace ancestors

---

## 1. Jurisdictional Context — Why It's Complicated

New Orleans in the early 1800s crossed **five overlapping governmental regimes**, each generating its own records in its own language:

| Period | Jurisdiction | Record Language(s) |
|---|---|---|
| 1718–1763 | French colonial | French |
| 1763–1800 | Spanish colonial | Spanish |
| 1800–1803 | French again (brief) | French |
| 1803–1812 | **Orleans Territory** (U.S.) | French + English |
| 1812 onward | **State of Louisiana** | English (French common) |

**Critical quirk — civil law, not common law:** Louisiana followed the Napoleonic Code, not English common law. This means the record universe is fundamentally different from every other U.S. state. Legal documentation centered on **notarial acts** rather than court filings; the jurisdiction is organized into **parishes**, not counties; and probate is called **succession**.

**The fires came before 1800 — but their shadow falls into your period.** The Great New Orleans Fire of **1788** destroyed roughly 856 buildings; the fire of **1794** destroyed another ~212 buildings in the newly rebuilt French Quarter. Both blazes consumed colonial civic structures and the records inside them. What you're working with in the early 1800s is partly a fresh start and partly what survived or was reconstructed.

**Ongoing disaster threats in the early 1800s:** Yellow fever epidemics (major outbreaks 1796, 1817, 1819, 1832), hurricanes, and repeated Mississippi River flooding continued to damage holdings throughout the period.

---

## 2. The Disaster Landscape — What Was Lost and When

### Pre-1800 fires (1788 and 1794)
- Destroyed the original French colonial governmental records held in civic buildings
- Spanish-era notarial archives and church registers in the Cathedral survived partially — Spain duplicated records and sent copies to Spain (see § 5 below)
- Many colonial-era land grants and judicial records were lost or burned

### Yellow fever epidemics (ongoing 1800–1850)
- Not a records-destruction event per se, but high-mortality years (1817, 1819, 1832 were severe) created surges in burial and succession records, some hastily recorded and later misfiled or lost
- Epidemic years also disrupted normal record-keeping by killing the record-keepers themselves

### Floods and hurricanes
- Recurrent flooding in the low-lying city damaged courthouses and archives periodically
- No single catastrophic flood-destruction event in this window is documented by the tools, but cumulative humidity and water damage degraded paper records throughout

### The 1862–1865 Union occupation and Civil War
- Falls outside the early 1800s but matters: the Union capture of New Orleans in 1862 saved the city from the burning that destroyed many other Southern archives. New Orleans record holdings are **substantially more intact than most Deep South cities** for this reason.

---

## 3. What Survived — Record by Record

### 🏛️ Church Records *(most important vital-events source for this period)*

**St. Louis Cathedral sacramental registers** (baptisms, marriages, burials) are the single most important genealogical resource for early New Orleans. They run from **1731 onward with gaps**, meaning they bracket the early 1800s entirely. The fires of 1788 and 1794 created gaps in the 1780s–1790s registers, but records resume for the period you are researching.

- **FamilySearch collection:** *Louisiana, Church Records, 1718–1925* (Collection ID [1612905](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1612905)) — **indexed with images**, ~987,654 records and ~493,827 images
- **Language warning:** Registers from 1800–1803 are in French; Spanish-era registers (pre-1800) are in Spanish; post-1812 registers shift toward English but French remains common into the 1840s. You must be able to read the original language or use a translation tool.
- **Also held at:** St. Louis Cathedral Archives, New Orleans; Diocese of Louisiana and the Two Floridas archives

### 📜 Notarial Records *(the most powerful substitute source in Louisiana)*

Because Louisiana's civil law tradition required a licensed notary to witness virtually every legal transaction — property sales, marriage contracts, wills, manumissions of enslaved people, business partnerships, apprenticeships, loans — the **notarial record is the nearest equivalent to court filings, deeds, wills, and contracts combined**.

**The New Orleans Notarial Archives Research Center (NONARC)** holds over **30 million pages** of notarial acts dating to 1734. This archive survived the fires and subsequent disasters because notarial offices maintained their own vaults, separate from civic buildings.

- **FamilySearch digitized volume:** Orleans Parish Notarial Acts, 1803–1820 (Image Group [004990115](https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/3:1:004990115)) — 167 images, in French and English
  - ⚠️ **Browse-only:** 0% name-indexed, not full-text searchable. Must be browsed image by image with no search capability.
- **For the full collection:** Contact NONARC directly for post-1820 notarial acts; much is undigitized
- **Why this matters as a substitute:** If a birth, marriage, or death record was destroyed, the notarial record of the marriage contract, will/succession, or property sale will often name spouses, parents, ages, and place of origin

### ⚰️ Probate / Succession Records

Louisiana calls probate **succession**. These records name heirs, inventory estates, and often provide ages and family relationships — a powerful substitute when vital records are missing.

- **FamilySearch collection:** *Louisiana, Probate Records, 1756–1984* (Collection ID [2078341](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/2078341)) — indexed with images, ~456,789 records, covering the full early 1800s window
- **Also held at:** Louisiana State Archives, Baton Rouge; Civil District Court, New Orleans
- **Tutorships and interdictions** (guardianship) are additional civil-law record types in the same archives — they name minor children and their parents

### 📋 Vital Registration (Civil)

Louisiana did **not** establish statewide civil registration of births and deaths until **1918** — one of the last U.S. states to do so. For the early 1800s, no equivalent of a state-issued birth certificate exists. Church registers are the primary vital-events source. For marriages:

- **FamilySearch collection:** *Louisiana, Parish Marriages, 1837–1957* (Collection ID [1798054](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1798054)) — indexed with images, ~567,890 records. Note: starts 1837, so this covers only the latter portion of the early 1800s window.
- For marriages before 1837, use the Church Records collection (above) and notarial marriage contracts

### 🗂️ Death Index

- **FamilySearch collection:** *Louisiana, Statewide Death Index, 1819–1964* (Collection ID [1543892](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1543892)) — indexed, ~2,345,678 records; no images. An index only, starts 1819.
- Before 1819: church burial registers are the only systematic death record

### 📊 Census Records

Federal census enumerations are available for the territory/state across the early 1800s:

| Year | Notes |
|---|---|
| 1805 | Orleans Territory census (territorial, not federal) |
| 1810 | First federal census after Louisiana Purchase |
| 1820 | Post-statehood federal census |
| 1830 | Federal |
| 1840 | Federal |
| 1850 | Federal — first to name all household members |

All available through **Ancestry.com** and **FamilySearch**. Note: 1890 federal census was destroyed by fire in Washington DC (1921), but that falls outside this period.

### ⚓ Passenger / Immigration Lists

New Orleans was the **second largest port of entry in the United States** after New York. Enormous volumes of immigrants arrived via the Port of New Orleans, especially from France, the German states, Ireland, and the Caribbean.

- **NARA** holds passenger arrival lists; many are indexed on **Ancestry.com** and FamilySearch
- **Newspapers.com** and **Chronicling America** (Library of Congress) carry port-arrival columns in early New Orleans newspapers (e.g., *Louisiana Gazette* from 1804, *Le Moniteur de la Louisiane* from 1794) listing passenger names

### 🪖 Military Records

- **War of 1812 / Battle of New Orleans (1815):** Service records and pension files at **NARA**; searchable on **Fold3**
- Louisiana militia records for the territorial period are at the **Louisiana State Archives**

### 🏞️ Land Records

- Federal land patents (BLM General Land Office, [glorecords.blm.gov](https://glorecords.blm.gov)) — Louisiana was a federal land state
- **Spanish land grants (pre-1803)** confirmed by U.S. Congress in 1811 — copies at NARA; originals in Spain
- Louisiana uses the **French arpent** measurement system in surveys, not the standard U.S. township/range system — a quirk that affects how you read and locate land records

### 📰 Newspapers

- Early New Orleans newspapers (*Louisiana Gazette*, *Le Moniteur de la Louisiane*, *Louisiana Courier*, *New Orleans Bee*) carry marriage notices, death notices, legal advertisements, and estate sale listings — often the only "vital record" for events in the 1800–1818 window
- **Newspapers.com**, **Chronicling America** (LOC), and the **New Orleans Public Library** hold holdings

---

## 4. FamilySearch Digitization Summary

| Collection | FS ID | Date Range | Records | Access Level |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Louisiana, Church Records | 1612905 | 1718–1925 | ~987,654 | ✅ Indexed + images |
| Louisiana, Parish Marriages | 1798054 | 1837–1957 | ~567,890 | ✅ Indexed + images |
| Louisiana, Probate Records | 2078341 | 1756–1984 | ~456,789 | ✅ Indexed + images |
| Louisiana, Statewide Death Index | 1543892 | 1819–1964 | ~2,345,678 | ✅ Index only (no images) |
| Orleans Parish Notarial Acts | Vol. 004990115 | 1803–1820 | 167 images | ⚠️ Browse-only (French + English) |

---

## 5. Substitute Sources When Records Are Missing

This is the heart of the question. When a record class is destroyed or simply never existed for the early 1800s:

| If this record is missing… | Try this instead |
|---|---|
| Birth/baptism | St. Louis Cathedral registers (FS Collection 1612905); notarial act of a marriage contract (names parents + ages) |
| Marriage | Notarial marriage contract at NONARC; church marriage register; newspaper announcement |
| Death/burial | Church burial register; succession (probate) filing triggered by death; newspaper death notice; cemetery inscription (St. Louis Cemetery No. 1, est. 1789; St. Louis Cemetery No. 2, est. 1823) |
| Vital records entirely destroyed | Notarial acts are the master substitute — they can reconstruct a life: birth implied in age at marriage contract, relationships named in succession, property history in sale acts |
| Spanish colonial records (pre-1803) lost in fires | **Archivo General de Indias, Seville, Spain** — Spain duplicated colonial records and shipped copies; significant Louisiana holdings survive there |
| French colonial records (pre-1763) | **Archives nationales d'outre-mer (ANOM), Aix-en-Provence, France** |
| General gap, any year | Federal census (1810, 1820, 1830, 1840, 1850); territorial census (1805) |

---

## 6. Key Repositories

| Repository | What It Holds | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **New Orleans Notarial Archives Research Center (NONARC)** | 30M+ pages of notarial acts, 1734–present | In-person and limited online; some volumes on FamilySearch |
| **Louisiana State Archives, Baton Rouge** | Territorial and early statehood records; militia; successions | In-person; many records on FamilySearch |
| **New Orleans Public Library — Louisiana Division** | City records, newspapers, photographs, directories | In-person; some digital |
| **Historic New Orleans Collection (HNOC)** | Manuscripts, maps, photographs, newspapers | In-person; online catalog |
| **Tulane University — Louisiana Research Collection** | Plantation records, family papers, church records | In-person |
| **St. Louis Cathedral Archives** | Sacramental registers 1731–present | In-person; duplicates on FamilySearch |
| **NARA Southeast Region (Atlanta)** | Federal records for Louisiana; passenger lists; War of 1812 | In-person; Fold3/Ancestry online |
| **Archivo General de Indias (Seville, Spain)** | Spanish colonial Louisiana duplicates | Online catalog; some scans at [pares.mcu.es](http://pares.mcu.es) |
| **FamilySearch.org** | Church records, probate, death index, notarial acts | Free online |

---

## 7. Critical Research Tips & Gotchas

1. **Search under "Orleans Parish," not a county.** Louisiana has no counties — it has parishes. Orleans Parish is the jurisdiction. Records indexed at the county level in other states will appear under the parish name here.

2. **The fires of 1788 and 1794 affect pre-1800 records, not early 1800s ones.** Researchers often assume New Orleans records are devastated. For the period you're researching (1800–1850), the baseline record survival is actually relatively good — especially because the city was not burned in the Civil War.

3. **Notarial records are not optional.** In Louisiana, the notary was the legal backbone of society. If your ancestor owned property, married, wrote a will, bought or sold anything, or was enslaved or freed, the transaction is likely in the notarial archives — often with more biographical detail than any vital record would provide.

4. **Expect French-language records well past 1812.** Louisiana statehood did not immediately shift record-keeping to English. Church registers and notarial acts in the early 1800s are frequently in French; some Spanish-language records persist from the colonial transition period. Plan to work with original-language documents.

5. **The 1805 Orleans Territory census** is a critical intercensal source — it predates the 1810 federal census and covers the early territorial period after the Louisiana Purchase.

6. **Look for Spanish colonial duplicate records in Spain** for ancestry that predates 1803. The Archivo General de Indias in Seville holds Louisiana colonial-era records sent from New Orleans during the Spanish period (1763–1800) — a completely separate cache of sources largely unknown to American researchers.

7. **The Port of New Orleans passenger lists** are vastly underused. For ancestors who arrived in the early 1800s, NARA's passenger records and the newspaper arrival columns are often the only immigration documentation.

8. **FamilySearch's notarial acts volume (004990115) covers only 1803–1820 and is browse-only** — 167 images in French and English with zero name-indexing. You must page through it manually. For post-1820 notarial research, contact NONARC directly.

---

## Coverage Gaps to Note Honestly

- The FamilySearch wiki pages for Louisiana and New Orleans returned only generic content in this session — **consult the FamilySearch Wiki directly** at [familysearch.org/wiki/Louisiana_Genealogy](https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Louisiana_Genealogy) for the most current guidance on specific collections.
- `volume_search` returned **only one digitized FamilySearch volume** for Orleans Parish in 1800–1850 (the notarial acts, 1803–1820). The vast majority of early New Orleans records are **not yet digitized on FamilySearch** and require in-person research at NONARC, the Louisiana State Archives, or the repositories listed above.
- Population data for Orleans Parish was not available in the FamilySearch dataset for this period.

---

**Bottom line for your research:** The disasters that most damaged New Orleans records happened *before* 1800. For the early 1800s, the survival picture is surprisingly good — but the records are unusual by American standards. The notarial archives and St. Louis Cathedral registers are your twin anchors. When either a birth, marriage, or death record is missing, a notarial act almost always fills the gap, and the entire NONARC collection stretches back to 1734.

output.text_response:
Now I'll run all the survey calls in parallel.I now have enough data from the tools to compile the guide. Here is what the survey found:

---

## 📋 Locality Research Guide: Port of Boston — 1840s Immigration

**Place:** Boston, Suffolk, Massachusetts, United States
**Time Period:** c. 1820–1870 (centered on the 1840s)
**Scope:** Immigrants entering through the Port of Boston and settling in Massachusetts

---

### Jurisdictional Context

Boston is the seat of **Suffolk County**, Massachusetts, which has existed as a jurisdiction since the colonial era. Massachusetts became a state in 1788. For the 1840s, all records were created under established state and county infrastructure. One critical threshold: **Massachusetts began statewide civil registration of births, marriages, and deaths in 1841** — so vital records for your immigrants exist in both town-level registers (pre- and post-1841) and statewide returns. The federal 1840 census names only the head of household; the **1850 census is the first to name every member** and record country of birth, making it a pivotal finding tool for immigrant families.

Boston in the 1840s was one of the five major U.S. ports of entry. It received a massive surge of **Irish Famine refugees** beginning around 1845–1852, as well as immigrants from Germany, England, and British Canada. By 1850, more than a quarter of Boston's residents were Irish-born (Wikipedia). Customs passenger lists — not the later detailed Ellis Island manifests — governed arrivals under the 1820 Steerage Act.

---

### Record-by-Record Survey

#### 1. 🚢 Port of Boston Customs Passenger Lists, 1820–1891
**The primary entry-point record for this research.**

| | |
|---|---|
| **FamilySearch collection** | [Massachusetts, Passenger Lists, 1820-1891](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1784312) (ID 1784312) |
| **Digitized volume found** | Image group `003345210` — 389 images, dated **1840–1850**, record type "Passenger Lists, Port of Boston" |
| **Digitization level** | ⚠️ **Nearly browse-only with full-text search.** Only **3% name-indexed** (not reliably reachable via name search). `fulltextSearchable: true` — so full-text keyword search is possible, but browsing image-by-image may also be required. |
| **Original repository** | **National Archives at Boston** (575 Endicott St., Waltham, MA), NARA Record Group 36 (Records of the U.S. Customs Service) |
| **Also on** | Ancestry.com (U.S. Customs Passenger Lists collection) |
| **Content warning** | 1840s manifests are sparse by modern standards: typically **name, age, sex, occupation, and country of origin only** — not hometown, parents' names, or destination address. Do not expect village-level origin data from the list alone. |

> **Quirk:** Because only 3% of this volume is name-indexed on FamilySearch, a name search may miss your ancestor entirely. Use the full-text search feature, or browse image-by-image when the full-text search fails.

---

#### 2. 📜 Massachusetts Town Vital Records, 1620–1890
| | |
|---|---|
| **FamilySearch collection** | [Massachusetts, Town Vital Records, 1620-1890](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/2061550) (ID 2061550) |
| **Coverage** | Births, marriages, and deaths recorded at the **town level**; Suffolk County/Boston records fall within this set |
| **Civil registration start** | **1841** — statewide registration began that year, but compliance was uneven in early years |
| **Also holds** | Pre-1841 church-transcribed and town-recorded vitals going back to the colonial period |

Use this to document immigrant children born in Boston shortly after arrival, marriages, and deaths. Early returns (1841–1845) may be incomplete.

---

#### 3. ⛪ Massachusetts Church Records, 1630–1920
| | |
|---|---|
| **FamilySearch collection** | [Massachusetts, Church Records, 1630-1920](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1921657) (ID 1921657) |
| **Key for Irish immigrants** | Catholic parish registers are **essential** — baptisms, marriages, and burials of Famine-era arrivals begin in the 1840s. The **Cathedral of the Holy Cross** and **St. Patrick's Church** (South Boston) are particularly important early parishes. |
| **Physical repository** | **Archdiocese of Boston Archives** holds original Catholic registers; some are on FamilySearch microfilm |
| **Protestant records** | Also documented newly arrived English, German, and Scots-Irish immigrants |

> **Quirk:** Civil registration (from 1841) and church registers overlap. For Irish Catholic immigrants, the parish register is often *more detailed* than the civil return — especially for baptisms, which may record the father's townland in Ireland.

---

#### 4. 📋 Massachusetts Naturalization Records, 1790–1906
| | |
|---|---|
| **FamilySearch collection** | [Massachusetts, Naturalization Records, 1790-1906](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1459213) (ID 1459213) |
| **Process** | Two-step: **Declaration of Intent** ("first papers," renouncing allegiance) filed any time after arrival; **Petition for Naturalization** ("second papers") filed ≥2 years later |
| **Key quirk** | **Pre-1906, any court of record could naturalize** — U.S. Circuit Court, U.S. District Court, Suffolk County Superior Court, Boston Municipal Court, or other local courts. Check all of them. |
| **Physical repositories** | **NARA at Boston** (federal court records); **Massachusetts State Archives** (state court naturalizations); individual county courthouse clerks |
| **Content** | May include country of birth, arrival port and date, age, and (in later filings) physical description — crucial for linking back to the passenger list |

> **Quirk:** Many 1840s immigrants did not naturalize for years or even decades after arrival. A naturalization dated 1855–1865 may still yield your ancestor's 1845 arrival details.

---

#### 5. 🗳️ Federal Census Records
| | |
|---|---|
| **1840 census** | Names **head of household only**; other members counted by age/sex brackets. Limited use for identifying individual immigrants |
| **1850 census** | ⭐ Names **every household member**, records age, sex, birthplace (country), and occupation — the first truly useful census for immigrant identification |
| **1860 census** | Adds real estate and personal property values; also names all members with birthplace |
| **Where** | FamilySearch (free) and Ancestry.com (subscription) both index all three censuses |
| **Mortality schedules** | 1850 and 1860 schedules record deaths in the 12 months preceding the census — useful for immigrants who died shortly after arrival |

---

#### 6. 📰 The Boston Pilot — "Missing Persons" Database
The *Boston Pilot*, an Irish Catholic newspaper, published thousands of **"Missing Persons" advertisements** placed by Irish immigrants searching for separated family members. These ads often name the county and townland of origin in Ireland. The database (1831–1921) is searchable free at **[infowanted.bc.edu](https://infowanted.bc.edu)** (Boston College). This is one of the most powerful tools for Irish Famine-era research and is not replicated anywhere else.

---

#### 7. 🏙️ Boston City Directories
Boston city directories survive from the early 19th century and were published annually. They list adult male residents (and some widows) by name, occupation, and address — useful for tracking an immigrant's movements year by year before and after the 1850 census. Many are digitized at the **Boston Public Library** and through Ancestry.com.

---

#### 8. 📰 Historical Newspapers
| | |
|---|---|
| **Newspapers.com** | Broad U.S. newspaper coverage including Boston-area papers |
| **Chronicling America** (Library of Congress) | Free digitized U.S. newspapers; some Massachusetts titles |
| **Boston Public Library** | Physical and microfilm runs of 19th-century Boston papers |

Arrival notices, death notices, and community announcements in Boston's Irish and mainstream press can supplement sparse official records.

---

#### 9. ⚔️ Military Records (Post-Arrival)
| | |
|---|---|
| **Fold3.com** | U.S. Civil War service records, pension files, and draft registrations (1861–1865) |
| **NARA** | Originals of all federal military records |

Many 1840s immigrants served in the Civil War; pension files in particular can contain detailed family history, place of birth in the old country, and arrival dates.

---

### Repository Summary

| Repository | What It Holds | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch.org** | Passenger lists (vol. 003345210), town vitals, church records, naturalization records | Free online |
| **National Archives at Boston (Waltham, MA)** | Original NARA RG 36 passenger lists; federal court naturalizations | In-person / some digitized |
| **Massachusetts State Archives (Boston)** | State court naturalizations, early vital records | In-person |
| **Archdiocese of Boston Archives** | Catholic parish registers (baptisms, marriages, burials) | In-person; some on FamilySearch |
| **Ancestry.com** | Passenger lists, federal censuses, city directories | Subscription |
| **Boston Pilot / infowanted.bc.edu** | Missing Persons ads for Irish immigrants | Free online |
| **Boston Public Library** | City directories, newspapers, local history | In-person / some digitized |
| **Newspapers.com** | Historical newspapers | Subscription |
| **Fold3.com** | Military records | Subscription |

---

### Key Research Tips & Quirks

1. **Start with the 1850 census, not the passenger list.** The census names everyone, gives country of birth, and often reveals when the family arrived (children born in Ireland vs. in the U.S. bracket the arrival year). Use it to anchor your timeline, then work backward to the passenger list.

2. **The passenger list volume is nearly all browse-only.** FamilySearch image group `003345210` (1840–1850) is only 3% name-indexed. Run a full-text search first; if that fails, browse the images directly for the approximate year of arrival.

3. **1840s manifests are thin.** They typically record only name, age, sex, occupation, and country of origin — not village, parents, or destination. The passenger list confirms arrival; the church register and naturalization record are where you find origin detail.

4. **Pre-1906 naturalizations can be in any court.** Do not limit your search to federal courts. Suffolk County, Boston Municipal Court, and other local courts all naturalized immigrants. FamilySearch collection 1459213 and NARA at Boston together cover the main series, but check the Massachusetts State Archives as well.

5. **Catholic parish registers often outperform civil registration for origin detail.** Baptismal entries in early Boston Catholic registers sometimes record the Irish townland or parish — detail that never appears in the civil return. Contact the Archdiocese of Boston Archives directly if the FamilySearch microfilm is incomplete.

6. **The Boston Pilot database is non-negotiable for Irish research.** If your ancestor was Irish and arrived during the Famine era, search [infowanted.bc.edu](https://infowanted.bc.edu) before concluding a search is exhaustive.

7. **Massachusetts civil registration began in 1841 — but early compliance was incomplete.** For events in 1841–1845, cross-check church registers even when a civil return is found (or not found).

---

### Coverage Assessment

| Record type | Digitization level |
|---|---|
| Port of Boston passenger lists, 1840–1850 | ⚠️ Full-text searchable; 3% name-indexed — nearly browse-only |
| Massachusetts passenger lists, 1820–1891 | Indexed collection on FamilySearch + Ancestry |
| Town vital records, 1620–1890 | Indexed on FamilySearch |
| Church records, 1630–1920 | Indexed on FamilySearch (partial); physical at Archdiocese |
| Naturalization records, 1790–1906 | Indexed on FamilySearch (partial); physical at NARA/State Archives |
| Federal census (1840, 1850, 1860) | Fully indexed on FamilySearch and Ancestry |
| Boston Pilot Missing Persons | Fully indexed (free, infowanted.bc.edu) |
| City directories | Partial digitization on Ancestry and BPL |
| Newspapers | Partial (Newspapers.com, Chronicling America) |

---

This is a well-documented period for Boston immigration research. The **1850 census + passenger list + naturalization record** form the core triangulation for establishing an immigrant's identity and origin. For Irish immigrants, add the **Boston Pilot database** and **Catholic parish registers** — together these four sources resolve the majority of Famine-era arrival questions.
