I'll start by resolving the place and establishing jurisdictional context for Indian Territory / Oklahoma in the mid-1800s, then survey all available records.Good — Indian Territory (pre-1907) maps to `Oklahoma, United States` for FamilySearch's record organization. Now I'll run the full parallel survey batch.Now let me read the specific Oklahoma Native American Records wiki page that surfaced from the search.I now have all the tool data I need. The wiki place pages returned only generic content — noting that as a coverage gap — but the collections, volume, wiki search, and external links results are substantive. Let me compile and present the guide.

---

# 🗺️ Locality Research Guide
## Indian Territory (Oklahoma) — Native American Tribal Research, Mid-1800s (c. 1830–1880)

---

### Jurisdictional Context

In the mid-1800s, present-day Oklahoma was federally designated **Indian Territory** — it did not become a U.S. state until **1907**. The Indian Removal Act of 1830 forced the relocation of the **Five Civilized Tribes** (Cherokee, Chickasaw, Choctaw, Creek, and Seminole) from the southeastern U.S. via the Trail of Tears. The federal government, Bureau of Indian Affairs (BIA), and tribal governments each created records throughout this period.

> **Critical search quirk:** FamilySearch organizes these records under **"Oklahoma, United States"** for its collections, but pre-1907 *volumes* are filed under **"Indian Territory, United States."** You must search under **both** place names across FamilySearch, Ancestry, and NARA to avoid missing records.

Civil registration of births, marriages, and deaths did **not** exist in Indian Territory during the mid-1800s. **Tribal enrollment rolls, federal agency records, church/mission registers, and land records are the primary genealogical substitutes.**

---

### Record Survey

---

#### 1. 📜 Tribal Enrollment Rolls *(HIGHEST PRIORITY)*

This is the cornerstone record class for this research.

**FamilySearch — "Oklahoma, Indian Censuses and Rolls, 1851–1959"**
- Collection ID: 1987432
- **567,890 records / 283,945 images** — covers the full target period
- Indexed and searchable at [familysearch.org/search/collection/1987432](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1987432)
- Includes annuity rolls, tribal censuses, and enrollment lists maintained by both the tribal governments and the BIA

**FamilySearch — Digitized Volume: Tribal Enrollment Cards, Indian Territory, 1840–1870**
- Image Group: 002298871 | **940 images**
- **99% name-indexed** and **full-text searchable** — highly accessible; searchable directly via `record_search` or `fulltext_search`
- Filed under "Indian Territory, United States" (not Oklahoma) in FamilySearch's volume system — search that place name specifically

**Specific named rolls (from FamilySearch Wiki — "Oklahoma Native American Records"):**

| Roll | Date | Tribe(s) | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Old Settler Cherokee Roll | 1851 | Cherokee | Pre-Dawes; lists Western Cherokee |
| Drennen Roll | 1852 | Cherokee | Post-removal enumeration |
| Dawes Rolls | 1898–1914 | All Five Tribes | Post-target period but essential — names family members, blood quantum, and references earlier rolls; indexed at FamilySearch and Ancestry |

> **Important:** The **Dawes Rolls** (1898–1914) postdate the mid-1800s but are the gateway record for proving tribal membership. They often cite parents and grandparents from the mid-1800s by name and blood quantum, making them indispensable for tracing backwards. The Dawes Rolls are held at the **National Archives, Fort Worth, Texas** and are indexed online at both FamilySearch and Ancestry.com.
>
> **Freedmen rolls** — parallel enrollment records for formerly enslaved people of the Five Tribes — are part of the same Dawes enrollment process and should be consulted if your ancestors may have been Freedmen or of mixed heritage.

---

#### 2. 🏛️ Federal Agency Records (Bureau of Indian Affairs)

*(Source: FamilySearch Wiki, "Oklahoma Native American Records — Missionary and Agency Records")*

The BIA maintained extensive records at each tribal agency:
- **Annuity payment rolls** — lists of tribal members who received treaty payments; essentially a census by another name
- **Agency correspondence** — letters naming individuals, disputes, family relationships
- **School enrollment records** — tribal boarding/day schools
- **Census-by-agency** — periodic headcounts at each of the Five Nations' agencies

**Repository:** These records are held at the **National Archives and Records Administration (NARA)** — primarily at **NARA Fort Worth (Southwest Region)**, which is the designated repository for Indian Territory / Five Civilized Tribes federal records.
- Access online: [archives.gov](https://www.archives.gov) *(confirmed in external links*); many have been microfilmed and some digitized on Fold3 and Ancestry

---

#### 3. 🌾 Land & Allotment Records

**FamilySearch — "Oklahoma and Indian Territory, Early Land Records, 1834–1907"**
- Collection ID: 1541872
- **234,567 records / 117,283 images**
- Covers treaty-era land grants, tribal allotments, and early patents
- Searchable at [familysearch.org/search/collection/1541872](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1541872)

Land records are especially valuable in Indian Territory because the federal government's allotment process required identifying tribal membership. The Dawes Commission land allotment files (part of the NARA Fort Worth holdings) contain family testimony that can supply parent-child and spousal relationships not found elsewhere.

---

#### 4. ⛪ Mission & Church Records

**FamilySearch — "Oklahoma, Mission and Church Records, 1820–1965"**
- Collection ID: 1654321
- **123,456 records / 61,728 images**
- Covers Baptist, Methodist, Presbyterian, and Moravian mission registers from the 1820s onward (per FamilySearch Wiki)
- Searchable at [familysearch.org/search/collection/1654321](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1654321)

Christian missionaries established churches and schools among the relocated tribes from the 1820s. Their registers — recording baptisms, marriages, and burials — are often the *only* vital-event records for this period. Some mission records name both Native names and Christian names adopted at conversion.

---

#### 5. 🪖 Military Records

The Civil War (1861–1865) cut through Indian Territory with significant tribal participation on both sides. The **Indian Home Guard** (Union) and Confederate-allied tribal units produced service records now held at NARA.

- **Fold3.com** — confirmed in external links; holds Civil War service records and pension files relevant to tribal soldiers
- Search for ancestors who served in units such as the 1st, 2nd, or 3rd Indian Home Guards (Union), or the Confederate Indian Brigade

---

#### 6. 📰 Newspapers

- **Newspapers.com** — confirmed in external links; holds historical newspapers
- The *Cherokee Advocate* (est. 1844) is a tribal newspaper published in both English and Cherokee that named tribal members and reported community events throughout the mid-to-late 1800s. Check Newspapers.com and the **Oklahoma Historical Society** for holdings.

---

#### 7. 🪦 Cemetery Records

- **FindAGrave.com** — confirmed in external links; tribal cemeteries in Oklahoma are indexed here, though mid-1800s coverage is uneven
- Tribal nations also maintain their own cemetery databases — contact the respective tribe's Historic Preservation Office

---

#### 8. 📋 Federal Census — Indian Schedules

The U.S. federal censuses of **1860 and 1870** included separate **Indian Schedules** for Indian Territory, which listed individuals by name and tribal affiliation. These are:
- Available on **Ancestry.com** (confirmed in external links)
- Some are browsable on FamilySearch
- Note: the standard population schedules largely did not enumerate tribal members living in Indian Territory — use the Indian Schedules specifically

---

### Repository Summary

| Repository | What It Holds | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch** (familysearch.org) | Indian Censuses & Rolls, Land Records, Mission Records, Tribal Enrollment Cards | Free online |
| **NARA Fort Worth** | BIA agency records, Dawes Commission files, land allotment case files, annuity rolls | In-person + partial online via Ancestry/Fold3 |
| **Ancestry.com** | Dawes Rolls (indexed), federal census Indian Schedules, BIA records | Subscription |
| **Fold3.com** | Civil War military service records (tribal units), pension files | Subscription |
| **FindAGrave.com** | Cemetery transcriptions | Free |
| **Newspapers.com** | Cherokee Advocate, Oklahoma territorial newspapers | Subscription |
| **Oklahoma Historical Society** (okhistory.org) | Tribal newspapers, territorial records, land records | In-person + partial online |

---

### Research Quirks & Warnings

1. **Dual place-name problem:** Search FamilySearch under both *"Oklahoma"* and *"Indian Territory"* — records are split across both place headings depending on when they were digitized.
2. **No civil registration:** There are no statewide birth/death certificates for Indian Territory before 1907. Tribal rolls, church registers, and annuity lists are the functional equivalents.
3. **Dawes Rolls are post-period but essential:** Even for mid-1800s research, start with the Dawes Rolls (1898–1914) to establish a family baseline and work backwards using blood quantum notations and parental names.
4. **Tribal citizenship ≠ modern enrollment:** Mid-1800s rolls reflect tribal citizenship under treaty-era rules, which differed by nation and changed over time. An ancestor may appear on one roll but not another due to band affiliation or political faction (e.g., Old Settlers vs. Emigrant Cherokee).
5. **Freedmen records run parallel:** If there is any possibility your ancestors were enslaved by tribal members, the Freedmen rolls and Freedmen census records in the same BIA/Dawes collections are equally important.
6. **FamilySearch wiki pages for Oklahoma** returned only generic content in this survey — consult the [FamilySearch Wiki for Oklahoma Native American Records](https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Oklahoma_Native_American_Records) directly for additional detail.

---

### Suggested Starting Sequence

1. **Start with the Dawes Rolls** — search FamilySearch collection 1987432 and Ancestry for your ancestor's name and tribe; note blood quantum and any parental names recorded
2. **Search the 1840–1870 Tribal Enrollment Cards volume** (imageGroupNumber 002298871 on FamilySearch) — 99% indexed, full-text searchable
3. **Pull BIA annuity rolls at NARA Fort Worth** for the relevant tribal agency in the 1830s–1870s
4. **Search mission church records** (FamilySearch collection 1654321) for baptism, marriage, and burial entries
5. **Check 1860 and 1870 federal census Indian Schedules** on Ancestry
6. **Consult the Oklahoma Historical Society** for the *Cherokee Advocate* and tribal newspapers

This sequence moves from the best-indexed, most accessible records toward the physical repositories — maximizing what you can accomplish online before an in-person NARA visit.

Let me start by establishing the jurisdictional context for Pennsylvania.Pennsylvania confirmed (standardPlace: "Pennsylvania, United States", state formed 1787). Now I'll run the full parallel survey batch.The wiki place pages returned generic content — I'll read the specific Pennsylvania Church Records page directly and search for more Quaker-focused wiki guidance.I now have everything needed to compile the guide. Here is the complete survey:

---

## 📋 Pennsylvania Quaker Research Guide — Late 1700s (ca. 1760–1800)

### Jurisdictional Context

Pennsylvania was founded in 1681 by William Penn, himself a Quaker, and the colony attracted large numbers of Friends from its earliest settlement. Pennsylvania became a state in 1787. **Critical for this period: Pennsylvania did not require statewide civil registration of births or deaths until 1906.** For late-18th-century Quaker families, meeting records *are* the vital records.

The three counties with the densest Quaker populations in the late 1700s were **Philadelphia**, **Chester** (est. 1682), and **Bucks** (est. 1682) — all original Penn counties. Lancaster County (est. 1729) also held significant Quaker communities.

---

### The Quaker Meeting Record System

Quakers organized records hierarchically — understanding this structure is essential before searching:

| Level | Function |
|---|---|
| **Preparative Meeting** | Local unit; prepared business for the Monthly Meeting |
| **Monthly Meeting** | **Primary record-keeping unit** — this is where vital records are found |
| **Quarterly Meeting** | Regional body consolidating several Monthly Meetings |
| **Philadelphia Yearly Meeting** | Governing body for PA, NJ, DE, and nearby areas |

**What Monthly Meeting registers contain — and why they're exceptional:**
- **Births & deaths** — recorded by the meeting, not a civil authority; these are the de facto vital records for the period
- **Marriages** — conducted "under the care of the meeting," not by a minister; marriage certificates were signed by *all witnesses present* (commonly 50–100 names), making them extraordinary genealogical documents
- **Certificates of Removal** — when a Friend moved to another meeting, they carried a signed certificate of good standing; these are the primary tool for tracing Quaker migration
- **Disownments** — disciplinary proceedings naming the individual and the offense; during the Revolution (1776–1783), *many* Pennsylvania Quakers were disowned for taking up arms; marrying a non-Quaker was another common cause
- **Meeting Minutes** — general business, naming elders, overseers, and members in various contexts

> ⚠️ **Quakers were legally exempt from civil marriage licensing in Pennsylvania.** Do not expect to find Quaker marriages in county courthouse bond/license collections.

---

### FamilySearch Holdings

**Indexed collections overlapping 1760–1800** (per `collections_search`):

| Collection | ID | Date Range | Records | Images | Access Level |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records | 1401638 | 1708–1985 | 1,245,830 | 607,220 | **Indexed + images** ✅ |
| Pennsylvania, Probate Records | 1417683 | 1683–1994 | 893,214 | 446,607 | **Indexed + images** ✅ |
| Pennsylvania, Civil Marriages | 1571957 | 1677–1950 | 312,045 | 156,023 | **Indexed + images** ✅ |
| Pennsylvania, Compiled Marriages | 1502909 | 1700–1900 | 198,765 | 0 | **Index only — no images** ⚠️ |

**Collection 1401638 ("Church and Town Records, 1708–1985") is your best starting point on FamilySearch** — it includes Quaker meeting registers and has 1.2 million name-indexed records covering this period.

**Digitized browse-only volume** (per `volume_search`):

| Volume | Image Group | Date Range | Images | Indexed? | Full-text? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Philadelphia Yearly Meeting Minutes | 001188322 | 1750–1800 | 145 | **0% — none** | **No** |

> ⚠️ This volume must be browsed image by image — there is no name index and no full-text search. Use it for elders, overseers, recorded ministers, and prominent Quaker families appearing in Yearly Meeting business.

**Coverage gap:** The `volume_search` returned only this single digitized volume for Pennsylvania 1760–1800. Individual Monthly Meeting registers for Chester, Bucks, and other key counties are held on FamilySearch microfilm but did not surface as online-accessible volumes in this survey. Search the **FamilySearch Catalog** directly by county and "Society of Friends" to locate the relevant film numbers for a specific meeting.

---

### Primary Repositories (Physical & Digital)

| Repository | Key Holdings | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Friends Historical Library, Swarthmore College** | Largest collection of American Quaker records; original meeting minutes, registers, and certificates of removal | First stop for any PA Quaker research (source: FamilySearch wiki, *Pennsylvania Church Records*) |
| **Haverford College Quaker & Special Collections** | Original Quaker manuscripts, meeting records | Strong complement to Swarthmore |
| **Historical Society of Pennsylvania (Philadelphia)** | Colonial and early national records; supplements meeting records | Especially strong for Philadelphia-area families |
| **Pennsylvania State Archives (Harrisburg)** | Land warrants/surveys from 1681; provincial records; tax assessment lists | Tax lists serve as census substitutes before 1790 |
| **County Register of Wills** | Probate records (wills, inventories, guardianships, administrations) from county formation | Filed at the county level |
| **County Courthouse / Deed Books** | Land transfers; marriage bonds (for non-Quakers and disowned Friends) | County-level; many microfilmed by FamilySearch |

---

### Complementary Record Types for 1760–1800

**Tax Assessment Lists (critical):**
- Annual tax duplicates survive from the 1690s in some PA counties; these are the best census substitutes before 1790 and for the decades between federal censuses
- Source: FamilySearch wiki, *Pennsylvania Land and Probate Records*

**Land Records:**
- Pennsylvania Land Office (Harrisburg): warrant, survey, and patent records from 1681
- County deed books begin at county formation (Philadelphia, Chester, Bucks all from 1680s)

**Probate Records:**
- County Register of Wills from the colonial period
- FamilySearch collection 1417683: 893,214 indexed records with images

**Federal Census:**
- The first federal census (1790) names only heads of household; all others listed by age/sex tally only
- For years before 1790, tax lists and Quaker meeting records are your primary population sources

**Published Abstracts — Hinshaw's Encyclopedia:**
- **William Wade Hinshaw, *Encyclopedia of American Quaker Genealogy*, 6 volumes** abstracts birth, marriage, death, and disownment records from PA Monthly Meeting minutes (source: FamilySearch wiki, *Pennsylvania Church Records*)
- Volume 2 covers Pennsylvania
- Available in print at major genealogical libraries; digitized on Ancestry.com; FamilySearch holds microfilm of source registers

---

### Research Strategy: Step-by-Step

1. **Start with Hinshaw** — search the *Encyclopedia of American Quaker Genealogy* first to identify which Monthly Meeting(s) your ancestors belonged to and to get an initial list of recorded events.

2. **Pursue the original Monthly Meeting registers** — contact Friends Historical Library (Swarthmore) or Haverford College for originals; FamilySearch collection 1401638 is the best name-searchable online alternative.

3. **Search the FamilySearch Catalog** by county + "Society of Friends" to locate microfilm rolls for individual meetings not yet digitized online.

4. **Follow Certificates of Removal** — these transfer documents are the primary tool for tracing Quaker migration between meetings and across colony/state boundaries.

5. **Check disownment minutes** — if an ancestor disappears from Quaker records in the 1776–1783 period, look for a disownment for military service; some later rejoined and are recorded again.

6. **Use tax assessment lists as a census substitute** before 1790 (and between enumerations); Pennsylvania State Archives and county courthouses hold these lists.

7. **Browse the Philadelphia Yearly Meeting Minutes** (imageGroup 001188322 on FamilySearch) for family members in meeting leadership; covers 1750–1800 in 145 images.

8. **Note the 1827 Hicksite–Orthodox split** — if research extends into the early 19th century, the separation divided meetings and created parallel record sets under two different branches.

---

**Summary:** Pennsylvania Quaker families in the late 1700s are exceptionally well-documented *if you know where to look.* Meeting records substitute for civil vital registration throughout this period. The Friends Historical Library at Swarthmore is the irreplaceable repository; FamilySearch collection 1401638 is the best name-indexed online starting point; and Hinshaw's *Encyclopedia* is the published finding tool that should precede any archive visit.

# Locality Guide: Pittsburgh / Allegheny County, Pennsylvania (c. 1865–1910)
*Focused on occupational and industrial sources for the steel industry era*

---

## Jurisdiction Overview

- **County formed:** 1788, carved from Westmoreland and Washington counties
- **County seat:** Pittsburgh (incorporated as a borough 1794; city 1816)
- **Parent jurisdiction:** Pennsylvania, United States
- **Economy:** Iron and steel manufacturing dominated from the 1870s onward. Carnegie Steel, Jones & Laughlin, Westinghouse, and dozens of smaller mills and fabricators employed the majority of the male workforce. The region also had coal mining, glass manufacturing, and river commerce.
- **Ethnic composition:** Large waves of Irish and German workers arrived first (1840s–1860s); Poles, Slovaks, Croatians, Italians, and Hungarians poured in from the 1880s through 1910, drawn specifically by the mills.
- **Religious denominations:** Roman Catholic (dominant among immigrant steelworkers, with ethnically organized parishes), Presbyterian, Methodist, Lutheran, Greek Catholic/Byzantine Catholic (Slovak and Ruthenian communities), Jewish congregations.
- **Key events for genealogists:** Homestead Steel Strike, 1892 (extensive court records and newspaper documentation of workers by name, occupation, and residence); great Pittsburgh flood, 1936 (post-period, but damaged some records); Pennsylvania statewide civil registration of births and deaths beginning January 1, 1906.

---

## Boundary Changes

- **City of Allegheny** (the North Side) was a **separate incorporated city** from Pittsburgh until **1907**, when it was annexed. Families living north of the Allegheny River before 1907 will appear in Allegheny City records — city directories, deed books, and local registers — *not* Pittsburgh city records. Always check both before assuming a gap.
- Allegheny County boundaries have been stable since formation in 1788; no county splits during the target period.
- Several mill towns surrounding Pittsburgh — Homestead, Braddock, McKeesport, Duquesne — are within Allegheny County and fall under its county-level records, but maintained their own borough/city directories and some local records.

---

## Available Record Types

### ⚙️ Occupational & Industrial Records *(the focus of this guide)*

This is the richest and most distinctive record class for Pittsburgh's steel era. Sources are scattered across several physical repositories — little is digitized or name-indexed.

| Source | What it contains | Repository | Access |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Company employment records** | Hiring dates, job titles, wages, department, sometimes ethnicity and birthplace | Heinz History Center (Pittsburgh); Rivers of Steel National Heritage Area archives | In-person; fragmentary survival |
| **Carnegie Steel / U.S. Steel records** | Selected records survive; Carnegie's personal papers at the Library of Congress | Heinz History Center; Library of Congress | In-person / online finding aids |
| **Union records — Amalgamated Association of Iron and Steel Workers** | Membership lists, local lodge rolls, grievance files | Historical Society of Western Pennsylvania (housed at Heinz History Center) | In-person |
| **State Factory Inspector Reports** | Annual Pennsylvania factory inspection reports list mills, worker counts, and sometimes worker names involved in accidents | Pennsylvania State Archives, Harrisburg; Carnegie Library Pittsburgh microfilm | Physical only |
| **City directories (occupational listings)** | Name, occupation, employer, street address — published annually | Carnegie Library of Pittsburgh has Pittsburgh directories from the **1810s** onward | In-person / some digitized on Ancestry and Internet Archive |
| **Federal census occupation columns** | 1870, 1880, 1900, and 1910 schedules all record occupation | FamilySearch (indexed + images); Ancestry | Indexed + images |
| **Homestead Strike records, 1892** | Congressional testimony, Allegheny County court records, and newspaper accounts name hundreds of workers by name, occupation, and residence | U.S. National Archives (congressional records); Allegheny County courthouse; Carnegie Library Pittsburgh newspaper files | Physical; some digitized via Newspapers.com |
| **OSHA predecessors / accident reports** | Coroner's inquest records document fatal mill accidents with worker identity | Allegheny County Coroner's Office records; Heinz History Center | Physical only |
| **Pension and benefit records** | Carnegie Relief Fund (forerunner of pension plans) maintained records of injured or retired steelworkers | Carnegie Mellon University archives | In-person |

**Coverage gap:** No centralized online database of Pittsburgh-area industrial employment records exists. The Heinz History Center and Rivers of Steel archives are the primary physical destinations. Call ahead or consult their online finding aids before visiting.

---

### Vital Records (Civil Registration)

- **Statewide registration began:** January 1, **1906** (births and deaths). Before that date Pennsylvania had **no statewide system**.
- **Allegheny County local registration:** Allegheny County began recording births and deaths locally around **1870** — earlier than most Pennsylvania counties. These pre-1906 Allegheny County registers are held at the **Allegheny County Health Department** and have been partially microfilmed by FamilySearch.
- **Death certificates, 1906–1968:** FamilySearch collection *Pennsylvania, Death Certificates, 1906–1968* — **4,218,763 records, fully indexed with images** (begins just after the core steel-boom period but covers the 1906–1910 tail and deaths of workers who lived into the 20th century).
- **For 1865–1905 vital events:** Church records are the primary alternative (see below). Also check the county's local registration registers for Allegheny County's 1870–1905 period.
- **Marriage records:** Kept at the county level throughout the period.
  - *Pennsylvania, County Marriages, 1885–1950* — **524,189 records, indexed + images** on FamilySearch
  - *Pennsylvania, Civil Marriages, 1677–1950* — **312,045 records, indexed + images** on FamilySearch
  - *Pennsylvania, Compiled Marriages, 1700–1900* — **198,765 records, index only, no images** on FamilySearch

---

### Church Records

**Critical source for immigrant steelworker families** — church registers often provide the only birth, marriage, and burial data for the 1865–1905 window.

- Pittsburgh's ethnic Catholic parishes were organized by nationality: Polish (St. Stanislaus, St. Adalbert), Slovak, Croatian, Italian, and Byzantine/Greek Catholic congregations each kept separate registers.
- FamilySearch collection *Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708–1985* — **1,245,830 records with 607,220 images**, indexed + images. Coverage is uneven; not all Allegheny County parishes are included.
- Unindexed parish registers are often still held by the **Diocese of Pittsburgh Archives** or at the individual parishes. Contact the Diocese for access.
- **Language note:** Records of Slovak, Polish, Croatian, and Italian parishes will often be in the heritage language or Latin. German Lutheran records may be in German.
- Presbyterian and Methodist records are generally in English and some are at the **Presbyterian Historical Society** (Philadelphia) or FamilySearch microfilm.

---

### Census Records

| Census Year | Notes | Access |
|---|---|---|
| 1870 Federal | Lists occupation; first census after Civil War to fully cover Pittsburgh's mill expansion | FamilySearch — indexed + images; Ancestry |
| 1880 Federal | Full household; occupation; birthplace of parents | FamilySearch — indexed + images; Ancestry |
| **1890 Federal** | **Largely destroyed by fire, 1921** — almost nothing survives for Allegheny County. Use city directories and tax records as substitutes for this decade. | Destroyed |
| 1900 Federal | Full household; immigration year; years in US; naturalization status | FamilySearch — indexed + images; Ancestry |
| 1910 Federal | Occupation listed; "industry" column added — directly names steel mills | FamilySearch — indexed + images; Ancestry |

**Pennsylvania did not conduct state censuses** during this period. The 1890 gap is significant — city directories (published annually) and Allegheny County tax assessment records are the best substitutes.

---

### Probate and Court Records

- **Probate (wills, administrations, inventories, guardianships):** Held at the **Allegheny County Register of Wills** from county formation (1788) onward.
- FamilySearch collection *Pennsylvania, Probate Records, 1683–1994* — **893,214 records with 446,607 images**, indexed + images at the state level. Allegheny County coverage within this collection should be verified by browsing.
- **Naturalization records:** Before 1906, a declaration of intent and petition for naturalization could be filed in *any* court of record. Allegheny County naturalizations are at the **Allegheny County courthouse** (Court of Common Pleas) and duplicates at the **National Archives at Philadelphia** (mid-Atlantic region). The National Archives holds the post-1906 records exclusively through the INS. Critically useful for immigrant steelworkers — naturalization papers list birthplace, arrival date, sometimes the ship.
- **Coroner's inquest records:** Mill accident inquests name the deceased worker with occupation, employer, witnesses. Held at the Allegheny County Coroner's Office; some transferred to the Heinz History Center.

---

### Land Records

- **Deeds:** Allegheny County Recorder of Deeds from 1788. Not typically a primary source for mill workers (who rented company housing or row houses), but relevant for those who achieved property ownership.
- **Tax assessment records:** Annual Allegheny County tax duplicates list taxable persons with property values and occupations. Valuable as a **1890 census substitute** and for tracking workers' economic mobility. Held at the **Allegheny County Assessment Office** and the **Pennsylvania State Archives**.

---

### Military Records

- **Civil War (1861–1865):** Many Pittsburgh-area men served. Pennsylvania regimental records at the **Pennsylvania State Archives**; federal service records and pension files at **NARA** and digitized on **Fold3** (subscription).
- **Spanish-American War (1898):** Some steelworkers served; records at NARA/Fold3.
- **WWI draft registration (1917–1918):** Covers all men born 1872–1900 — captures the generation of workers who arrived as immigrants in the 1880s–1900s. Indexed + images on Ancestry and FamilySearch. These cards list employer and occupation, making them doubly useful for occupational research.

---

### Cemetery Records

- **Major cemeteries:** Allegheny Cemetery (non-sectarian, est. 1844), Calvary Cemetery (Catholic), St. Mary's Cemetery, numerous ethnic Catholic parish cemeteries.
- **FindAGrave.com** has extensive Allegheny County coverage — searchable online, free.
- **Physical sexton records** vary by cemetery; contact individual cemeteries for burial registers predating FindAGrave coverage.

---

### Newspaper Records

Pittsburgh newspapers are a major resource for steelworker families — labor disputes, accidents, immigration arrivals, and obituaries all appear regularly.

| Newspaper | Coverage | Access |
|---|---|---|
| *Pittsburgh Post-Gazette* (various predecessors) | 1786–present | Newspapers.com (subscription); Carnegie Library Pittsburgh microfilm |
| *Pittsburgh Press* | 1884–1992 | Newspapers.com; Carnegie Library microfilm |
| *Homestead Daily Messenger* and local mill-town papers | Late 1800s–early 1900s | Carnegie Library Pittsburgh; limited digitization |
| Ethnic-language press (Polish, Slovak, Croatian papers) | Various founding dates in the 1880s–1900s | Carnegie Library Pittsburgh; Balch Institute / Temple University |

**Newspapers.com** (subscription) is the primary online portal for digitized Pittsburgh papers. The **Carnegie Library of Pittsburgh** holds the most complete microfilm run for local and ethnic papers.

---

### Immigration and Naturalization Records

- **Ports of entry:** Most Eastern and Southern European steelworkers arrived at **New York (Ellis Island, 1892+)** or **Baltimore**. Some also entered via Philadelphia.
- **Passenger lists:** Ellis Island records (1892–1957) are fully indexed and free at **Ancestry.com** and **FamilySearch**. Baltimore arrivals are on FamilySearch. Lists show name, age, last residence, destination, and (after 1906) physical description.
- **Naturalization records:** Pre-1906 — any court; post-1906 — federal courts only. Allegheny County naturalizations at the county courthouse and NARA Philadelphia. Search indexes at Ancestry; originals at NARA.
- The 1900 and 1910 federal censuses record immigration year and years in the United States — use these to estimate arrival windows before searching passenger lists.

---

## Repository Guide

### Online Repositories

| Repository | Key collections for Pittsburgh / Allegheny County | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch** (familysearch.org) | Church & Town Records 1708–1985 (1.2M records); County Marriages 1885–1950; Probate Records 1683–1994; Death Certificates 1906–1968; federal census 1870–1910 | Free |
| **Ancestry.com** | Federal census (all years); PA vital records; city directories; Ellis Island passenger lists; Fold3 military records | Subscription |
| **Newspapers.com** | Pittsburgh Post-Gazette, Pittsburgh Press, and regional papers | Subscription |
| **Fold3.com** | Civil War service/pension, WWI draft cards, Spanish-American War | Subscription |
| **FindAGrave.com** | Allegheny County cemetery transcriptions | Free |
| **NARA Access to Archival Databases (AAD)** | Post-1906 naturalization indexes | Free |

### Physical Repositories

| Repository | Key holdings | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **Heinz History Center** (Pittsburgh) | Carnegie Steel records; union records; city directories; immigration records; Allegheny County historical collections | In-person; research appointments recommended |
| **Rivers of Steel National Heritage Area** (Homestead) | Mill-specific industrial records; Homestead Strike materials | In-person; contact for appointments |
| **Carnegie Library of Pittsburgh** (Oakland) | City directories 1810s–present; local newspapers on microfilm; ethnic press; local history collection | In-person; free |
| **Allegheny County Courthouse** | Marriage licenses (from formation); Register of Wills (probate); Court of Common Pleas (naturalization pre-1906) | In-person; some indexes online |
| **Pennsylvania State Archives** (Harrisburg) | State factory inspection reports; land records; Civil War records; state-level vital records | In-person; online finding aids at digitalarchives.state.pa.us |
| **National Archives at Philadelphia** (mid-Atlantic region) | Federal naturalization records; federal court records; NARA mid-Atlantic regional holdings | In-person / written request |
| **Diocese of Pittsburgh Archives** | Parish registers not on FamilySearch microfilm | Contact Diocese for access |
| **Carnegie Mellon University Archives** | Carnegie Relief Fund records; university-related industrial history | In-person; by appointment |

---

## Records NOT Online

The following records **exist but are not available in any online database** — do not assume they are lost simply because they don't appear in a search:

- **Company employment records** (Carnegie Steel, J&L, Westinghouse) — physical only at Heinz History Center and Rivers of Steel; survival is fragmentary
- **State factory inspector reports listing individual workers**
- **Coroner's inquest records** for mill accident victims
- **Ethnic-language parish registers** not yet microfilmed by FamilySearch
- **Allegheny County local birth/death registers, 1870–1905** — partially microfilmed; not fully indexed online
- **Pre-1906 naturalization originals** at the Allegheny County courthouse
- **City of Allegheny records (North Side pre-1907)** — partially distinct record set from Pittsburgh city records
- **Annual tax assessment duplicates** at Allegheny County

---

## Known Record Losses

- **1890 federal census:** Almost entirely destroyed by fire in the Commerce Department building in Washington, D.C. in 1921. Essentially no Allegheny County schedules survive. **Substitutes:** City directories (published annually by Pittsburgh city printers), Allegheny County tax assessment lists, church membership rolls.
- **No record of catastrophic courthouse fire** for Allegheny County — county records have relatively good survival compared to many Pennsylvania counties, though individual record sets may have gaps.

---

## Research Tips

1. **Start with the federal census** for any decade you're interested in (1870, 1880, 1900, 1910). The occupation column will confirm steel/mill employment and the employer's mill town, narrowing which company records to pursue.

2. **The 1890 gap:** Use Pittsburgh city directories (annually at Carnegie Library and Ancestry) and Allegheny County tax assessment records to bridge the 1880–1900 gap. City directories list occupation and home address, helping you track a worker's mobility between mills.

3. **Identify the ethnic parish first.** For Polish, Slovak, Croatian, Italian, and Hungarian steelworkers, the ethnic Catholic or Greek Catholic parish register is almost certainly the only source for births and deaths before 1906. Determine your ancestor's nationality, then find the corresponding Pittsburgh parish — the Diocese of Pittsburgh Archives can identify which parish served which neighborhood.

4. **City of Allegheny (North Side) before 1907:** If your family lived on the North Side, check records under "Allegheny City" or "Allegheny, Pennsylvania" — not "Pittsburgh" — in all directories, deed indexes, and local records predating 1907 annexation.

5. **Naturalization is pivotal for immigrant workers.** Pre-1906 declarations of intent and petitions filed at the Allegheny County Court of Common Pleas name birthplace, arrival date, and ship. Check both the county courthouse index and NARA Philadelphia, since some workers filed in federal court.

6. **Homestead Strike, 1892, as a name source:** Congressional testimony from the 1892 hearings (published and available through HathiTrust and the Library of Congress) names hundreds of Homestead workers with their positions and wages. Even if your ancestor wasn't a strike leader, the surrounding community lists are valuable.

7. **WWI draft registration cards (1917–1918)** are an underused occupational source. They identify the worker's employer by name — often a specific steel mill — and capture the immigrant generation who arrived in the 1880s–1900s. Available indexed + images on Ancestry and FamilySearch.

8. **Rivers of Steel as a starting point for mill-specific research:** Contact Rivers of Steel (riversofsteel.com) for guidance on which company records survive for a specific mill (Homestead, Braddock, Duquesne, South Side). They can often direct you to a specific finding aid before you make an in-person trip.

9. **Pennsylvania death certificates begin January 1, 1906** — workers who died after that date will have a certificate listing birthplace, parents' names, and occupation, making these the richest vital documents in the collection for immigrant steelworker families.

---

*Sources: FamilySearch Wiki — "Allegheny County, Pennsylvania Genealogy" (Industrial Records and Immigration Records sections); FamilySearch Wiki — "Pennsylvania Vital Records"; Wikipedia — "Pittsburgh"; Wikipedia — "Homestead Strike"; FamilySearch collections search (Pennsylvania scope, 1850–1910).*

---

# Locality Guide: Philadelphia, Pennsylvania — 1793 Yellow Fever Epidemic

## Jurisdiction Overview

- **Established:** Philadelphia County formed 1682 by William Penn as one of the three original Pennsylvania counties; the City of Philadelphia occupied a smaller footprint within the county in 1793 (city and county did not consolidate until the Act of 1854)
- **Administrative center:** Philadelphia (city seat and county seat)
- **Parent jurisdiction:** Pennsylvania (Commonwealth)
- **Population during period:** Approximately 50,000 residents in 1793 (per Wikipedia, *Yellow Fever Epidemic of 1793*); roughly 5,000 — one-tenth of the population — died during the outbreak; thousands more fled to surrounding counties
- **Significance:** Philadelphia was the capital of the United States in 1793, making it the nation's largest and most politically prominent city
- **Key event:** The yellow fever epidemic ran August–November 1793; Mayor Matthew Clarkson and physician Benjamin Rush left detailed accounts; the Committee Appointed by the Citizens of Philadelphia administered relief efforts and generated its own documentary record

---

## Boundary Changes

Philadelphia County's boundaries were stable and well-established by 1793 — no boundary changes directly affect research for this period. The 1854 Consolidation Act merged the City of Philadelphia with the surrounding county townships, but this postdates the epidemic by over 60 years. **Important caveat:** thousands of Philadelphians fled the epidemic into neighboring Chester, Delaware, and Montgomery counties — so records of refugees may surface in *those* jurisdictions' churches, tax lists, and court records, not in Philadelphia County records at all.

---

## The Epidemic as a Genealogical Event

The 1793 outbreak is unusual because it generated its own documentary record *independent of* the normal vital-records infrastructure. Three source categories are unique to this event:

1. **Mathew Carey's published account** — *A Short Account of the Malignant Fever* (1793, multiple editions) contains printed lists of named victims. This is a published secondary source compiled in real time and is one of the most direct name-finding tools for the epidemic. Held at the Library Company of Philadelphia and the Historical Society of Pennsylvania; digitized copies are available via HathiTrust and Google Books.

2. **Committee of Citizens minutes and reports** — A citizens' committee (chaired by Mayor Clarkson) organized food, medicine, and burials during the crisis. Its minutes and ledgers record individuals who received relief, names of the dead, and family circumstances. Manuscript originals are held at the **Philadelphia City Archives** and the **Historical Society of Pennsylvania (HSP)**. *(Source: FamilySearch Wiki, "Philadelphia Genealogy — Yellow Fever Epidemic of 1793")*

3. **Bush Hill Hospital records** — The city's improvised epidemic hospital at Bush Hill generated patient registers. These manuscript records are held at the **HSP**. They are physical-only; no online digital version has been confirmed in tool results.

---

## Available Record Types

### Church Records ⭐ *Primary source for 1793 vital events*

Pennsylvania had **no civil vital registration in 1793** — statewide registration did not begin until 1906. Church registers of baptisms, marriages, and burials are therefore the *only* systematic vital records for families in this period.

Philadelphia's extraordinary religious diversity means you must identify your family's denomination before searching:

| Denomination | Key congregation(s) | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Anglican / Episcopal | Christ Church (est. 1695), St. Peter's | Christ Church burial register records epidemic deaths |
| Lutheran | St. Michael's & Zion | German-language records |
| German Reformed | First Reformed | German-language records |
| Presbyterian | First Presbyterian, Second Presbyterian | |
| Roman Catholic | St. Mary's, Holy Trinity | |
| Quaker (Friends) | Philadelphia Monthly Meeting (multiple) | Exceptionally detailed; separate men's and women's minutes |
| Swedish Lutheran | Gloria Dei (Old Swedes', est. 1677) | Oldest surviving church in Pennsylvania |
| Jewish | Congregation Mikveh Israel (est. 1740) | |
| African Methodist Episcopal | Mother Bethel (founded 1793 by Richard Allen) | AME records relevant to Black Philadelphians who stayed and nursed the sick |

**FamilySearch collection:** *Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708–1985* (collection ID 1401638) — **1,245,830 records**, 982,415 persons indexed, 607,220 images. **Digitization level: indexed + images** for a significant portion, though not all denominations are equally covered. Search this first. *(URL: https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1401638)*

Many colonial and early national Philadelphia church registers have been transcribed and published in print. The **Genealogical Society of Pennsylvania (GSP)** has compiled indexes to many Philadelphia church records; their publication series *Pennsylvania Genealogical Magazine* contains transcripts of registers from Christ Church, Gloria Dei, St. Peter's, and others. *(Source: FamilySearch Wiki, "Philadelphia Genealogy — Church Records")*

**Quaker records** deserve special mention: the Society of Friends maintained exceptionally detailed monthly meeting records — births, marriages ("certificates"), deaths, disownments, and removal certificates — in separate men's and women's minute books. Philadelphia Quarterly Meeting encompassed multiple monthly meetings. Originals are held at **Haverford College's Quaker & Special Collections** and **Friends Historical Library at Swarthmore College**; many have been microfilmed by FamilySearch and are included in the Church and Town Records collection.

> **Language note:** Lutheran and German Reformed registers from this period are in German. If your family was German, plan for translation.

---

### Probate Records ⭐ *Especially valuable for 1793 deaths*

The epidemic killed ~5,000 people in roughly three months, creating a surge of estate filings. Probate records — wills, letters of administration, inventories, and distribution accounts — name heirs, name executors (often relatives), and may describe real and personal property.

**FamilySearch collection:** *Pennsylvania, Probate Records, 1683–1994* (collection ID 1417683) — **893,214 records**, 893,214 persons indexed, 446,607 images. **Digitization level: indexed + images.** *(URL: https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1417683)*

**Physical repository:** Philadelphia Register of Wills (City Hall Annex, Philadelphia) holds original volumes. The **Philadelphia City Archives** also holds probate-related court records.

> **Tip:** Even if an ancestor died intestate (without a will), an administration filing may exist naming the surviving spouse, children, and creditors. In epidemic years, intestate filings far outnumber wills — search both.

---

### Census Records

| Census | Year | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Federal census | **1790** | First U.S. census — taken three years *before* the epidemic; names only heads of household; establishes pre-epidemic household composition |
| Federal census | **1800** | Taken seven years *after* the epidemic; compares household composition to 1790, revealing deaths and family breakup |
| Mortality schedules | 1850–1885 | Too late for 1793; not applicable |
| State census | None | Pennsylvania held no state census in this period |

The 1790 and 1800 federal censuses are **indexed + images** on both FamilySearch and Ancestry. Comparing a family across both enumerations can reveal who survived the epidemic and who did not — but heads of household only; other members are counted by age/sex brackets, not named.

> **Gap-filler:** Philadelphia city directories were published from **1785** onward (*Macpherson's Directory*, *Clement Biddle's Directory*, *Hardie's Philadelphia Directory*, etc.). These are essentially annual censuses of householders and can bridge the gap between 1790 and 1800. Held at the Library Company of Philadelphia and HSP; many are digitized via HathiTrust and Ancestry.

---

### Vital Records (Civil Registration)

**Pennsylvania did not have statewide civil registration in 1793.** A state registration law was enacted in 1852 (Act of 1852) but compliance was poor and inconsistent; statewide death registration did not become reliable until after 1906. **For 1793, there are no civil death, birth, or marriage certificates.** Church records (above) are the substitute.

*Pennsylvania, Death Certificates, 1906–1968* (collection ID 2255317) — does not cover 1793.

---

### Land and Tax Records

Land deeds, mortgages, and property tax records can locate families in the city even when vital records are absent. They can confirm a person was alive (or dead) at a specific date.

- **Philadelphia County deed books** (1682–) — held at the **Philadelphia City Archives**. Some early volumes are available on FamilySearch microfilm. Deeds often name adjacent landowners and family relationships.
- **Pennsylvania tax lists** — State and county property tax assessments exist for this period. The **Pennsylvania State Archives** (Harrisburg) holds many county tax records. Some Philadelphia tax lists from the 1790s have been indexed and published by genealogical societies.
- **Digitization level:** Partial — some deed volumes on FamilySearch microfilm; tax lists largely physical-only or microfilm.

---

### Newspaper Records ⭐ *Especially useful for epidemic death notices*

Philadelphia had an active press in 1793. During the epidemic, several papers published lists of the dead, notices of business closures, refugee destinations, and estate/legal notices. Key titles:

| Paper | Period | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| *Pennsylvania Gazette* | 1728–1815 | Major paper; epidemic death notices |
| *Federal Gazette* | 1788–1798 | Published during epidemic; death lists |
| *Dunlap's American Daily Advertiser* | 1791–1795 | Daily coverage of the epidemic |
| *General Advertiser* (later *Aurora*) | 1790–1824 | Benjamin Franklin Bache's paper |

**Access:** Many Philadelphia newspapers from this period are digitized on **Newspapers.com**, **Chronicling America** (Library of Congress, free), and via **ProQuest Historical Newspapers**. Originals and microfilm are held at HSP and the Library Company of Philadelphia.

> **Research tip:** Obituaries and death notices in 1793 Philadelphia papers are a primary tool for confirming epidemic deaths — especially for middle- and upper-class families. Estate notices ("all persons indebted to the estate of…") appear for weeks after a death and can confirm the date of death.

---

### Military Records

The 1793 epidemic does not directly intersect with active military conflict, but context records exist:

- **Revolutionary War** (1775–1783) — Many 1793 adults served; pension files (NARA) and bounty land warrants give rich biographical detail, often including wives' names and residences
- **NARA (Philadelphia regional archives)** — Holds pension files, service records, and naturalization records for the Philadelphia area
- **FamilySearch:** *Pennsylvania, Compiled Military Records* and associated collections; **Fold3** holds Revolutionary War pension files (subscription)

---

### Cemetery Records

During the epidemic, normal burial procedures broke down — coffins ran short, graves were dug hastily, and some victims were buried in mass or improvised graves. Many deaths went unrecorded in parish registers simply because clergy were themselves dead or fled. This is a genuine record gap.

Established cemeteries active in 1793:

| Cemetery | Affiliation | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Christ Church Burial Ground (est. 1719) | Episcopal | Many notable burials; some epidemic victims |
| Gloria Dei (Old Swedes') Churchyard | Lutheran/Episcopal | One of oldest burial grounds in PA |
| St. Peter's Churchyard | Episcopal | |
| Friends burial grounds | Quaker | Multiple locations (Northern Liberties, etc.) |
| Mikveh Israel Cemetery (est. 1740) | Jewish | Oldest Jewish cemetery in PA |
| Potter's Field | Municipal | Pauper burials; minimal records |

**FindAGrave** (findagrave.com) and **BillionGraves** have indexed some Philadelphia church burial grounds; coverage is partial and inconsistent for the 1790s. Sexton records and burial registers, where they survive, are held by the individual churches or their successor congregations.

---

### Court and Naturalization Records

Pre-1906 naturalizations could be filed in any court of record — federal, state, or local. Philadelphia courts (Court of Common Pleas, Supreme Court of Pennsylvania) hold early naturalization records. The **Philadelphia City Archives** and **NARA Philadelphia** are the primary repositories. Some naturalization records from this period are on FamilySearch microfilm.

---

## Repository Guide

### Online Repositories

| Repository | Relevant collections | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch** | *PA Church & Town Records 1708–1985* (1401638); *PA Probate Records 1683–1994* (1417683); *PA Civil Marriages 1677–1950* (1571957); *PA Compiled Marriages 1700–1900* (1502909); 1790 & 1800 federal census | Free |
| **Ancestry.com** | 1790 & 1800 federal census (indexed + images); Philadelphia city directories; some church records | Subscription |
| **Newspapers.com** | Philadelphia newspapers 1793 | Subscription |
| **Chronicling America** (Library of Congress) | Some Philadelphia papers from this period | Free |
| **HathiTrust / Google Books** | Mathew Carey's *Short Account* (1793); published church register transcripts | Free |
| **Fold3** | Revolutionary War pension files (for pre-epidemic context) | Subscription |
| **NARA (archives.gov)** | Federal records, naturalization, pension files | Free finding aids; some images on Fold3/Ancestry |

### Physical Repositories

| Repository | Key holdings | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **Philadelphia City Archives** (City Hall Annex) | Committee of Citizens records; deed books; later vital records; court records | In-person; some records by appointment |
| **Historical Society of Pennsylvania (HSP)** | Bush Hill hospital records; Committee of Citizens manuscripts; church record transcripts; newspapers; manuscript collections | In-person (members); some digitized |
| **Library Company of Philadelphia** | Mathew Carey's *Short Account*; Philadelphia newspapers 1793; city directories | In-person; some digitized |
| **Genealogical Society of Pennsylvania (GSP)** | Church record indexes; *Pennsylvania Genealogical Magazine* transcripts | Membership / in-person |
| **Philadelphia Register of Wills** | Original probate volumes (wills, administrations, inventories) | In-person |
| **Pennsylvania State Archives (Harrisburg)** | County tax lists; early state records | In-person; some online via PA State Archives portal |
| **Haverford College — Quaker & Special Collections** | Philadelphia-area Quaker meeting records | In-person |
| **Friends Historical Library, Swarthmore College** | Philadelphia Quaker records | In-person |
| **NARA — Mid-Atlantic Region (Philadelphia)** | Federal court records; naturalization; Revolutionary War pensions | In-person |

---

## Records NOT Online

The following categories exist but are **not fully available in any online database** and require physical or microfilm access:

- **Bush Hill Hospital patient registers** — manuscript, held at HSP; physical only
- **Committee of Citizens manuscript minutes** — held at HSP and Philadelphia City Archives; physical only (though Mathew Carey's printed summary is digitized)
- **Philadelphia County deed books** for this period — partial microfilm on FamilySearch; originals at Philadelphia City Archives
- **Most Philadelphia church burial registers** from the 1790s that are not in the FamilySearch Church & Town Records collection — held by individual congregations or diocesan archives
- **Philadelphia County tax assessments, 1790s** — largely physical or microfilm at Pennsylvania State Archives
- **Philadelphia city directories, 1785–1800** — digitized on HathiTrust and some Ancestry; not universally indexed by name across all years

---

## Known Record Losses and Gaps

- **No civil vital registration in 1793** — there are simply no Pennsylvania birth or death certificates for this year. Church records are the substitute; if a family had no church affiliation or their congregation's register does not survive, the death may be unrecorded except in newspapers or the Carey account.
- **Epidemic burial disruption** — the scale of deaths overwhelmed churches; some burials were not recorded even in church registers that otherwise survive. Accept that some families will leave no burial record from August–November 1793.
- **Fled residents** — thousands who left Philadelphia during the epidemic appear in no Philadelphia record for the duration. Look in Chester, Montgomery, Bucks, and Delaware county records for the same period.
- **No Philadelphia-specific courthouse fire** — unlike many Pennsylvania counties, Philadelphia's main repositories generally survived intact. The primary record loss is the *incomplete creation* of records during the epidemic itself, not post-facto destruction.

---

## Research Tips

1. **Start with the Mathew Carey account.** *A Short Account of the Malignant Fever* (1793) is the fastest single source for confirming whether a named individual died in the epidemic. Multiple editions were printed; the third and fourth editions have the most complete death lists. Free on HathiTrust.

2. **Work church records by denomination, not geography.** Philadelphia had dozens of congregations. Identify the family's religion first; then search the specific congregation's register rather than browsing the broad FamilySearch collection.

3. **Mine probate records aggressively for 1793.** An unusual surge in estate filings that year reflects the epidemic's death toll. Even a modest estate generates an administration record that names survivors.

4. **Use city directories to bridge the census gap.** The 1790 and 1800 censuses bracket the epidemic, but city directories (annual) let you track whether a householder was still present year-by-year.

5. **Check surrounding counties for refugees.** Many who survived did so by leaving. Friends and relatives in Chester, Montgomery, Bucks, and Delaware counties may have hosted epidemic refugees — look for Philadelphia-born individuals appearing suddenly in those counties' 1793–1794 records.

6. **Consult the Genealogical Society of Pennsylvania's published indexes.** Their *Pennsylvania Genealogical Magazine* contains transcribed and indexed Philadelphia church registers that are not fully indexed on FamilySearch. The GSP also maintains manuscript indexes at their Philadelphia research room.

7. **For Black Philadelphian families:** The Free African Society (founded 1787) and the African Methodist Episcopal Church (Mother Bethel, founded 1793) were central to the epidemic response. AME church records and Free African Society minutes are the primary sources for African American Philadelphians of this period.

8. **The FamilySearch** *Pennsylvania, Compiled Marriages, 1700–1900* **(collection 1502909) has 198,765 records but zero images** — it is an index only, with no underlying digitized document. Treat any match as a pointer to the original church register, not a confirmed record in itself.

---

*Sources: FamilySearch Wiki, "Philadelphia Genealogy — Yellow Fever Epidemic of 1793" and "Philadelphia Genealogy — Church Records"; Wikipedia, "Yellow Fever Epidemic of 1793"; FamilySearch collections search (Pennsylvania scope, 1750–1830); FamilySearch external links survey.*

output.text_response:
I'll begin by establishing the jurisdictional context for Chicago, Illinois, then run a full parallel survey of available records. Let me start with the place lookup and a Wikipedia search on the Great Chicago Fire.Chicago's `standardPlace` is **Chicago, Cook, Illinois, United States**. The Wikipedia summary confirms the Cook County Courthouse was destroyed in the fire, wiping out many official records. Now I'll run the full parallel survey.All tools loaded. Now issuing the full parallel survey in a single batch:The wiki search returned a specific Cook County wiki page. Let me read it now.Now I have everything I need. Let me compile the full guide.

---

## Locality Research Guide
### Chicago / Cook County, Illinois — Great Chicago Fire, 1871

---

### Jurisdictional Context

**Place:** Chicago, Cook County, Illinois, United States
**County formed:** Cook County, established 1831 from Putnam County
**The disaster:** The Great Chicago Fire burned October 8–10, 1871, killing ~300 people and leaving more than 100,000 homeless. The **Cook County Courthouse burned**, destroying the city's official pre-fire record archive — deeds, probate files, court records, birth and death registers, and tax rolls. *(Wikipedia: "Great Chicago Fire"; FamilySearch Wiki: "Cook County, Illinois Genealogy — Record Loss and the Great Fire")*

> ⚠️ **The most important fact for this research:** Almost all Cook County official records created *before* October 1871 no longer exist. This is not a digitization gap — the records themselves were destroyed. Research into pre-fire families requires substitute sources throughout.

---

### Records Available — By Type

#### 1. Vital Records (Birth, Marriage, Death)

Cook County civil registration began **after** the fire. The following indexed collections are on FamilySearch:

| Collection | Records | Date Range | Access |
|---|---|---|---|
| Illinois, Cook County, Birth Certificates | 1,450,000 | **1871–1940** | Indexed — name-searchable |
| Illinois, Cook County, Death Records | 980,000 | **1871–1947** | Indexed — name-searchable |
| Illinois, Cook County, Marriage Records | 620,000 | **1871–1920** | Indexed — name-searchable |

**Critical gap:** All three collections begin in 1871 — there are no surviving Cook County civil vital records for events *before* the fire. Pre-1871 births, marriages, and deaths must be sought in **church registers** (see below).

**Repository for originals:** Cook County Clerk's Office; FamilySearch holds digitized copies.

---

#### 2. Church Records

**FamilySearch collection:** *Illinois, Cook County, Chicago, Church Records, 1833–1925* — **285,000 records, indexed and name-searchable.**

This is the **primary substitute source for pre-1871 vital events**. Some parishes maintained records in central denominational archives outside Chicago and thus survived the fire. Key repositories:
- **Archdiocese of Chicago, Joseph Cardinal Bernardin Archives** — Catholic parish registers, some pre-dating the fire; access by appointment
- **Denominational archives** (Methodist, Lutheran, Presbyterian, etc.) — held outside the courthouse, many survived
*(FamilySearch Wiki: "Cook County, Illinois Genealogy — Repositories")*

---

#### 3. Property / Land Records (Including Burnt Record Act Reconstructions)

Two digitized volumes on FamilySearch (from volume search):

| Volume | Image Group | Images | Indexed | Dates | Record Type | Access |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pre-fire reconstructed court records | 009982110 | 38 | **0%** | 1865–1871 | Reconstructed pre-fire copies | **Browse-only — image by image, no search** |
| Post-fire property re-recordings | 009982111 | 112 | **41%** | 1871–1880 | Burnt Record Act re-recordings | Partially indexed; remainder browse-only |

**The Burnt Record Act (1872)** required property owners to re-register their deeds after the fire. These re-recordings are the primary substitute for destroyed pre-fire deed books. Note: only properties whose owners actively re-registered are included — those who died, fled, or lacked resources may not appear.

**Chicago Title and Trust Company records** — compiled pre-fire property ownership to support title reconstruction; now held at the **Chicago History Museum**. A vital substitute source for pre-fire land and property research. *(Wikipedia: "Great Chicago Fire"; FamilySearch Wiki)*

**Federal land patents** (BLM General Land Office, glorecords.blm.gov) — original federal patents predate the courthouse and were never housed there; they survive intact.

---

#### 4. Federal Census Records — Survived the Fire ✅

Federal census originals were held in Washington, D.C. and were **not affected by the fire**. The **1870 census** is the single most valuable surviving snapshot of Chicago families immediately before the fire.

| Census Year | Coverage | Access |
|---|---|---|
| 1850 | Cook County families | Indexed — FamilySearch, Ancestry |
| 1860 | Cook County families | Indexed — FamilySearch, Ancestry |
| **1870** | **Chicago families just before the fire** | **Indexed — FamilySearch, Ancestry** |
| 1880 | Post-fire recovery population | Indexed — FamilySearch, Ancestry |

*(FamilySearch Wiki: "Cook County, Illinois Genealogy — Record Loss and the Great Fire")*

---

#### 5. City Directories

Chicago city directories survive from the **1840s onward** and are held at the **Newberry Library**. *(FamilySearch Wiki: "Cook County, Illinois Genealogy")*

These list heads of household, occupations, and addresses year by year. A family appearing in the 1870 directory and absent in 1872 — or present in both — is itself genealogical evidence about fire survival. Partial digitization available on Ancestry.com and the Internet Archive.

---

#### 6. Historical Newspapers

Chicago newspapers documented the fire in exhaustive detail — publishing casualty lists, missing persons notices, homeless relief recipient lists, and property loss notices. These can directly name fire victims and survivors.

| Newspaper | Access |
|---|---|
| *Chicago Tribune* (Oct–Dec 1871) | Newspapers.com (subscription); Chicago History Museum archives |
| *Chicago Times*, *Inter-Ocean* | Newspapers.com; Chronicling America (Library of Congress, free) |

---

#### 7. Military Records — Unaffected by Fire ✅

For men who served in the Civil War (1861–1865) — a large cohort among 1871 Chicago adults — military records survive at the federal level.

| Record Type | Repository | Access |
|---|---|---|
| Service records, pension files, bounty land warrants | NARA | Fold3 (subscription); NARA online |
| State adjutant general records | Illinois State Archives | In-person and some online |

---

#### 8. Immigration and Naturalization

Pre-1906 naturalizations were filed in any court of record. **Cook County pre-fire naturalization records are destroyed.** However:
- **NARA passenger lists** (arriving at ports of entry) survive — not affected by the fire
- **Post-1871** Cook County naturalizations survive
- **U.S. District Court for Northern Illinois** records were not housed in the county courthouse and may survive for federal-level naturalizations

**Access:** NARA Great Lakes Region (Chicago); Ancestry.com; FamilySearch

---

#### 9. Insurance / Title Records (Key Substitute Source)

The **Chicago Title and Trust Company** assembled records of pre-fire property ownership to support post-fire title reconstruction. Their collection at the **Chicago History Museum** is one of the most important substitute sources for pre-fire property and family research and is not replicated elsewhere. *(Wikipedia: "Great Chicago Fire")*

---

### Key Repositories at a Glance

| Repository | What They Hold | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch** | Church records (285K), birth/death/marriage (post-1871), census, military, property re-recordings | Free — familysearch.org |
| **Chicago History Museum** | Chicago Title & Trust insurance/title records, fire-era personal papers, photographs, relief records | In-person; some digitized |
| **Newberry Library** | City directories (1840s–), maps, manuscripts, newspapers | In-person; some digitized online |
| **IRAD – Northeastern Illinois Univ.** | Cook County official records post-1871 | In-person |
| **Archdiocese of Chicago, Bernardin Archives** | Catholic parish registers (some pre-fire) | By appointment |
| **Cook County Clerk's Office** | Civil vital records (births, marriages, deaths from 1871) | In-person and limited online |
| **NARA Great Lakes Region (Chicago)** | Federal records: passenger lists, naturalizations, federal court records | In-person; some digitized |
| **Illinois State Archives** | State-level records; land grants; Civil War state military records | In-person and some online |
| **BLM General Land Office** | Federal land patents (pre-fire, unaffected) | Free — glorecords.blm.gov |
| **Ancestry.com** | Census, city directories, some Cook County collections | Subscription |
| **Newspapers.com** | Historical Chicago newspapers | Subscription |
| **Fold3** | Military records | Subscription |

---

### Recommended Research Order for Fire-Affected Families

1. **1870 federal census** — documents the family just before the fire; fully indexed on FamilySearch and Ancestry
2. **1880 federal census** — documents survival and reconstitution after the fire
3. **Chicago Tribune, Oct–Dec 1871** — casualty lists, missing persons, relief recipient lists (Newspapers.com)
4. **City directories, 1869–1874** (Newberry Library) — year-by-year presence/absence brackets the impact
5. **Church records** (FamilySearch: 285,000 records, 1833–1925) — substitutes for destroyed civil vital records
6. **Burnt Record Act re-recordings** (FamilySearch vol. 009982111, 1871–1880; 41% indexed) — confirms post-fire property re-establishment
7. **Chicago History Museum** — insurance/title records for pre-fire property; fire relief papers
8. **Military records** (Fold3, NARA) — Civil War veterans; unaffected by the fire
9. **BLM GLO** — federal land patents; not in the courthouse, fully intact

---

### Key Gaps to Know

| Gap | Impact |
|---|---|
| Pre-1871 civil vital records | **Entirely destroyed — no workaround.** Church registers are the only substitute. |
| Pre-fire probate, wills, estate files | Destroyed. No substitute for the specific contents. |
| Pre-fire naturalizations (county court) | Destroyed. Federal court and post-1871 county records may partially compensate. |
| Pre-fire reconstructed court records (FamilySearch vol. 009982110) | **Browse-only** — 0% indexed, not full-text searchable; must be examined image by image. |
| Burnt Record Act coverage | Incomplete — covers only properties actively re-registered by surviving owners. |

---

> **Quirk to remember:** The 1870 federal census for Chicago survived because the original schedules were retained in Washington, D.C. — not locally. This makes it uniquely valuable as the last complete enumeration of Chicago families before the fire. Always search it first.
>
> **Second quirk:** The FamilySearch Illinois wiki place pages returned only generic content during this survey. For Illinois- and Cook County–specific guidance, consult the FamilySearch Wiki article **"Cook County, Illinois Genealogy"** directly at `familysearch.org/en/wiki/Cook_County,_Illinois_Genealogy`.
