---

# Locality Guide: Louisiana — French Colonial Period (1700s, ca. 1699–1769)

## Jurisdiction overview

- **Colonial authority:** France (La Louisiane), 1682–1762. Spain took nominal possession in 1762 (Treaty of Fontainebleau) but did not exercise physical control until 1769 under Governor Alejandro O'Reilly. The 1700s therefore spans **French colonial (1699–1769)** and the opening of Spanish colonial rule.
- **Administrative center:** New Orleans, founded 1718 by Jean-Baptiste Le Moyne de Bienville.
- **Governing bodies:** The Superior Council (Conseil Supérieur) was the civil/legal authority for French Louisiana — it issued land grants, heard court cases, recorded contracts, and administered estates.
- **Legal system:** French civil law (not English common law). This produced a distinct notarial record tradition that survives to the present day.
- **Religion:** Under the **Code Noir** (Black Code), Catholicism was the only legally recognized religion; all persons — including enslaved people — were required to be baptized Catholic. Catholic parish registers are therefore the principal genealogical source for this period.
- **Language of records:** French throughout the French colonial period; transitioning to Spanish after 1769.
- **Population data:** No population figures were returned by the MCP tools for this period.

## Boundary changes

- **1699–1762:** French colonial territory — no U.S. state/county subdivisions. The colony was organized into military posts and districts (e.g., New Orleans, Mobile, Natchitoches), not parishes or counties.
- **1762:** Treaty of Fontainebleau — France ceded Louisiana west of the Mississippi to Spain; the eastern portion went to Britain.
- **1769:** Spain assumes physical control. Records shift from French to Spanish administration; notarial and church records continue but switch language.
- **1800–1803:** Brief French control, then the Louisiana Purchase. U.S. Territory of Orleans created 1803; Louisiana statehood 1812.
- **Implication for researchers:** There are no county-level records for the 1700s. All records were created at the colonial/territorial level, held centrally in New Orleans or in France. Do not search by Louisiana parish or county when targeting this period — search the colonial-era repositories directly.

---

## Available record types

### Church records — **PRIMARY SOURCE for this period**

- **Denominations:** Catholic only (legally mandated under Code Noir).
- **Record types:** Baptisms, marriages, burials. Crucially, baptism registers include enslaved persons by name — often the only documentary record of enslaved individuals in this period.
- **Coverage on FamilySearch:** **Louisiana, Church Records, 1718–1925** (Collection ID 1612905; ~987,654 records, ~493,827 images). This collection begins with the first St. Louis Cathedral registers from 1720, per the FamilySearch wiki. This is the single most important FamilySearch collection for 1700s Louisiana research.
- **Access level:** Indexed + images (FamilySearch, free). Note: early 18th-century entries are in archaic French script — the researcher must read the original language.
- **Physical holdings:** Archdiocese of New Orleans Archives; Louisiana Historical Center (Old U.S. Mint building, 400 Esplanade Ave., New Orleans).
- **Gaps:** Records prior to 1718 are extremely sparse. The New Orleans fires of 1788 and 1794 destroyed portions of early records; some gaps exist in the surviving registers.

### Civil vital records

- **Start date:** Civil registration did **not** exist during the French colonial period. Louisiana's first statewide death index begins in 1819 (after statehood), and civil marriage registration at the parish level begins even later. **Church records are the sole equivalent** for births (baptisms), marriages, and deaths (burials) in the 1700s.
- **FamilySearch note:** Louisiana, Parish Marriages, 1837–1957 (coll. 1798054) and Louisiana, Statewide Death Index, 1819–1964 (coll. 1543892) both postdate the French colonial period and are not applicable as primary sources for the 1700s.

### Superior Council (Conseil Supérieur) records

- **Types:** Land grants (concessions), court proceedings, contracts, slave sale records, estate inventories, civil and criminal cases.
- **Date range:** 1699–1769 (French colonial era).
- **Where held:** Louisiana Historical Center, located in the Old U.S. Mint building, New Orleans — partially digitized, per the FamilySearch wiki (*Louisiana Genealogy*, "French Colonial Records" section). Researchers may also access through the Louisiana State Museum system.
- **Access level:** Partially digitized; physical access or correspondence with the Louisiana Historical Center required for undigitized portions.
- **Language:** French.

### Notarial records

- **Overview:** The New Orleans Notarial Archives is the **oldest continuous notarial office in North America**, with records from the 1730s onward, per the FamilySearch wiki (*Louisiana Land and Property*, "Colonial Land Grants" section).
- **Types:** Contracts, wills, marriage settlements (contrats de mariage), property sale transactions, manumissions.
- **Where held:** New Orleans Notarial Archives Research Center — public access. Address: 1340 Poydras St., Suite 1300, New Orleans, LA.
- **Access level:** Physical access; some records have been microfilmed or digitized in part.
- **Language:** French (1730s–1769); Spanish from 1769.
- **Research value:** Marriage contracts, especially, contain detailed family information — names of parents, witnesses, and dowry arrangements — often surpassing what appears in church registers.

### Probate and estate records

- **FamilySearch collection:** **Louisiana, Probate Records, 1756–1984** (Collection ID 2078341; ~456,789 records, ~228,394 images). The 1756 start date overlaps the last years of French colonial rule.
- **Access level:** Indexed + images (FamilySearch, free). Browse-only or microfilm status of the earliest volumes is not determinable without `volume_search` data (tool not available in this environment — check individual volumes on FamilySearch for indexing detail).
- **Note:** Colonial estate inventories are also part of the Superior Council records at the Louisiana Historical Center and may predate the FamilySearch collection's 1756 start.

### Land records and colonial concessions

- **French system:** Land was granted as *concessions* (not under the rectangular federal survey system). Records follow French civil law tradition.
- **After the Louisiana Purchase (1803):** Many colonial-era French and Spanish land claims were re-adjudicated by the U.S. government. These proceedings are documented in the **American State Papers: Public Lands** series (available free at memory.loc.gov and via Ancestry/HathiTrust). Volume 3 covers Louisiana land claims and names many families whose original grants date to the French colonial period.
- **Louisiana State Land Office** (Baton Rouge) holds original colonial land grant documentation.
- **Access:** American State Papers are fully digitized and free online. Louisiana State Land Office records require direct contact.

### Colonial census rolls

- **Authority:** The French colonial government conducted periodic population counts (*recensements*). These are held at the **Archives Nationales d'Outre-Mer (ANOM)**, Aix-en-Provence, France, along with administrative correspondence, per the FamilySearch wiki.
- **Access:** ANOM has an online portal (anom.archivesnationales.culture.gouv.fr) with partial digitization; some census rolls have been transcribed by researchers and published. Not available on FamilySearch or Ancestry.
- **Language:** French.
- **Research value:** Colonial census rolls can establish household composition, ages, and relationships for families not yet traceable in other record types.

### Military records

- **Relevant units:** French colonial troops (Compagnies Franches de la Marine) served in Louisiana throughout the 1700s.
- **Where held:** ANOM in France holds the primary French colonial military records. **Fold3** (fold3.com — subscription) holds some U.S.-era military records.
- **Access level:** ANOM records are physical/partially digitized in France. Fold3 covers mainly post-1803 U.S. military records.

### Cemetery records

- **Online coverage:** FindAGrave (findagrave.com) covers some Louisiana cemeteries, but 18th-century colonial-era burials are sparsely represented. Most burials are documented only in Catholic burial registers (see Church Records above).
- **Physical:** St. Louis Cemetery No. 1 in New Orleans (established 1789) is the oldest surviving cemetery; earlier burials were in church grounds that have not always survived.

### Newspapers

- **Coverage gap:** No Louisiana newspapers were published during the French colonial period. The first Louisiana paper, *Moniteur de la Louisiane*, began in 1794 — after the Spanish took control. Newspapers.com (listed in FamilySearch's curated external links for Louisiana) does not cover the 1700s French colonial period.

### Immigration / passenger records

- **Context:** Most French settlers arrived via France or Canada (New France). Ship passenger lists for the 1718–1720 founding-era immigration (the "casket girls" and other organized immigration schemes) have been transcribed and published. The founding-era passenger lists are not on FamilySearch but are available in published genealogical works and at the Louisiana Historical Center.
- **NARA** (archives.gov — listed in FamilySearch's curated external links) holds federal-era records from 1803 onward, not French colonial arrivals.

---

## Repository guide

### Online repositories

| Repository | Relevant holdings | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch** | Louisiana, Church Records, 1718–1925 (coll. 1612905); Louisiana, Probate Records, 1756–1984 (coll. 2078341) | Free |
| **ANOM (France)** | Colonial administrative correspondence, census rolls, military records for French Louisiana | Free portal; partial digitization |
| **Ancestry.com** | U.S. Federal Census (post-1803); some Louisiana databases | Subscription |
| **Fold3** | Military records (mainly post-1803 U.S. era) | Subscription |
| **Library of Congress** (memory.loc.gov) | American State Papers: Public Lands — colonial land claim adjudications | Free |

### Physical repositories

| Repository | Holdings | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **Louisiana Historical Center** (Old U.S. Mint, New Orleans) | Superior Council records (civil/legal, 1699–1769); partially digitized | In-person; contact Louisiana State Museum system |
| **New Orleans Notarial Archives Research Center** | Notarial records from 1730s onward | In-person; public access |
| **Archdiocese of New Orleans Archives** | Catholic parish registers; sacramental records | By appointment |
| **Louisiana State Land Office** (Baton Rouge) | Colonial land grants and concessions | In-person / written request |
| **Archives Nationales d'Outre-Mer (ANOM)**, Aix-en-Provence, France | Colonial census rolls, administrative correspondence, military records | In-person; partial online portal |
| **Louisiana State Archives** (Baton Rouge) | State-era records (post-1812); some territorial records | In-person / online request |

---

## Records NOT online

- **Superior Council proceedings** (the bulk of French colonial civil/legal records) — only partially digitized; the Louisiana Historical Center holds undigitized portions requiring in-person or mail access.
- **Colonial census rolls (recensements)** — held at ANOM in France; not available on FamilySearch, Ancestry, or other major U.S. platforms.
- **French colonial military rosters** (Compagnies Franches de la Marine) — at ANOM; not on U.S. platforms.
- **New Orleans Notarial Archives pre-1803 volumes** — largely not name-indexed online; require in-person access at the Research Center.
- **Founding-era immigrant passenger lists (1718–1720)** — published in specialized genealogical works; not in a searchable online database.

---

## Known record losses

- **New Orleans fires of 1788 and 1794** destroyed portions of early colonial records, including some parish registers and civil documents. These are significant gaps for the mid-to-late French colonial period. Researchers should note when a family or event falls in the period immediately around these fires and seek corroborating records at ANOM or in notarial records that may have survived.
- **Dispersal across two countries:** Because France ceded Louisiana before the records were ever consolidated in one place, colonial records are split between New Orleans (Louisiana Historical Center) and Aix-en-Provence, France (ANOM). A thorough search requires consulting both repositories.

---

## Research tips

1. **Start with FamilySearch Collection 1612905 (Church Records, 1718–1925).** This is the most accessible entry point for French colonial family research and covers baptisms, marriages, and burials. Records are in French — plan for translation of archaic 18th-century script.

2. **The Superior Council records are indispensable for non-church events.** Land transactions, estate inventories, civil disputes, and manumissions appear here. Contact the Louisiana Historical Center directly about what has and has not been digitized.

3. **Use the New Orleans Notarial Archives for marriage contracts and property.** A *contrat de mariage* (marriage contract) from this period can name both sets of parents, grandparents, and witnesses — far richer than a church register entry alone. Records begin in the 1730s.

4. **Check ANOM for colonial census rolls.** The *recensements* can place a family in a specific district and give household composition. The ANOM online catalog (anom.archivesnationales.culture.gouv.fr) is searchable in French.

5. **The Code Noir means enslaved ancestors appear in Catholic registers.** Baptism entries for enslaved persons are in the same registers as free persons and typically give the enslaver's name, location, and sometimes the enslaved person's African origin or creole birthplace.

6. **American State Papers: Public Lands resolves land lineage post-1803.** Many colonial French land claims were confirmed (or disputed) in U.S. proceedings after the Louisiana Purchase. A land-holding family in the 1700s almost certainly has a traceable claim in the American State Papers, which name the original grantee and heirs.

7. **Records transition from French to Spanish in 1769.** If your ancestor's trail crosses the 1769 line, expect records to shift language and administrative system. Spanish colonial records (1769–1803) are held at the same repositories — Louisiana Historical Center and ANOM — but are in Spanish and follow Spanish notarial conventions. The Archivo General de Indias (Seville, Spain) also holds Louisiana Spanish colonial administrative records.

8. **No volume_search data was available** for this survey, so the browse-only vs. indexed status of individual volumes within the two overlapping FamilySearch collections (1612905 and 2078341) could not be determined. Check each collection's volume list on FamilySearch before planning searches, as early 18th-century volumes may be browse-only images requiring page-by-page review.

---

**Sources used:** Wikipedia, "French Louisiana"; FamilySearch Wiki, "Louisiana Genealogy — French Colonial Records (1699–1762)"; FamilySearch Wiki, "Louisiana Land and Property — Colonial Land Grants"; FamilySearch `collections_search` (Louisiana, United States, 1700–1800); FamilySearch `external_links_search` (Louisiana, United States, 1700–1800).

---

# Locality Guide: Pennsylvania Coal Mining Towns (c. 1850–1910)

## Jurisdiction overview

- **Region covered:** Primarily the **Anthracite Coal Region** — Schuylkill, Luzerne, Lackawanna, Carbon, and Northumberland counties in northeastern Pennsylvania. The bituminous coal counties of western PA (Allegheny, Westmoreland, Fayette, Washington) share many of the same record types.
- **Parent jurisdiction:** Commonwealth of Pennsylvania (state since 1787)
- **County seats:** Pottsville (Schuylkill), Wilkes-Barre (Luzerne), Scranton (Lackawanna), Jim Thorpe (Carbon), Sunbury (Northumberland)
- **Population during period:** The region grew dramatically 1870–1900 driven by immigration; no census population data was returned by the tool for the specific period.
- **Economy:** Anthracite coal extraction (northeastern PA); company towns and colliery operations dominated community life.
- **Ethnic composition (per Wikipedia, *Coal Region*):** Early waves — Irish, Welsh, German (1840s–1870s). Later waves — Polish, Lithuanian, Slovak, Italian, Ukrainian (1880s–1900s).
- **Religious denominations:** Irish and Polish Roman Catholic, Welsh Calvinistic Methodist (Congregational), Lithuanian Catholic, Slovak Catholic, German Lutheran, Welsh Baptist, Eastern Orthodox.
- **Key historical events:** Molly Maguires trials (1876–77); Great Railroad Strike (1877); Lattimer Massacre (1897); founding of the United Mine Workers of America (UMWA, 1890); massive immigration surges through the 1880s–1900s.

---

## Boundary changes — Critical for Lackawanna County researchers

- **Lackawanna County was carved from Luzerne County in 1878.** The Scranton, Carbondale, and surrounding areas were part of Luzerne County before that date. All records for these localities before 1878 — deeds, probate, court records, naturalization — were created and filed under **Luzerne County**. Search Luzerne County first for pre-1878 events in the Scranton area; do not treat these as two unrelated record sets but as one continuous trail split by the 1878 formation.
- Other primary coal county boundaries were generally stable during the 1850–1910 period.

---

## Available record types

### Vital records (civil registration)

⚠️ **Critical gap:** Pennsylvania did **not** require statewide civil registration of births and deaths until **1906**. During the entire late-1800s target period, the coal counties had **no mandatory birth or death registration**. These records were simply never created — they were not lost, they do not exist. Church records are the only substitute.

- **Exception:** Philadelphia recorded births from ~1860; Allegheny County from ~1870 — but the anthracite coal counties (Schuylkill, Luzerne, Lackawanna, Carbon, Northumberland) did not.
- **Death Certificates from 1906:** FamilySearch "Pennsylvania, Death Certificates, 1906–1968" — **4,218,763 records with images, fully indexed** (collection 2255317). Starts after the target window but will capture deaths of people born in the late 1800s.
- **Marriage records** — kept at the county level from the colonial period. Three FamilySearch collections cover this:
  - *Pennsylvania, County Marriages, 1885–1950* — 524,189 records, 312,045 images (collection 1921317) — **indexed + images**; overlaps the late target period
  - *Pennsylvania, Civil Marriages, 1677–1950* — 312,045 records, 156,023 images (collection 1571957) — **indexed + images**
  - *Pennsylvania, Compiled Marriages, 1700–1900* — 198,765 records, no images (collection 1502909) — **indexed, no images**

### Church records — Most important source for pre-1906 vital events

Church registers are the **primary substitute for civil birth and death records** throughout the target period. Ethnic parishes kept baptism, marriage, and burial registers that are often the only documentation of vital events.

- **Denominations present:** Irish and Polish Catholic, Welsh Calvinistic Methodist/Congregational, Lithuanian Catholic, Slovak Catholic, German Lutheran, Welsh Baptist, Eastern Orthodox
- **Record types:** Baptisms, marriages, burials, confirmations, communicant rolls
- **FamilySearch access:** *Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708–1985* — **1,245,830 records, 607,220 images, indexed** (collection 1401638). One of the most valuable collections for this period.
- **Language warning:** Welsh-language registers for Welsh Congregational and Baptist congregations; Polish, Lithuanian, Slovak, or Latin for Catholic ethnic parishes. Researchers must be prepared to read or translate non-English registers.
- **Gap:** Many local parish registers have NOT been microfilmed or digitized and remain at individual churches or diocesan archives. Contact the **Diocese of Scranton** (Lackawanna area) and the **Diocese of Allentown or Altoona** (Schuylkill and surrounding counties) for Catholic records not yet in FamilySearch.

### Census records

- **Federal census:** 1860, 1870, 1880, and 1900 are fully available and indexed. The 1890 census was largely destroyed by fire — see Known Record Losses below.
- **Mortality schedules (1860–1885):** Record deaths in the year preceding the census; can document deaths during the entire pre-1906 gap in civil registration. Available on FamilySearch and Ancestry.
- **Occupation field:** Federal census schedules for 1870–1900 list occupation as "miner," "mine laborer," or "collier," making it straightforward to identify mining families in specific townships. (Per FamilySearch Wiki, *Pennsylvania Occupational Records — Coal Mining Records.*)
- **Access:** FamilySearch (free) and Ancestry.com (subscription).

### Occupational records — Distinctive to coal mining

This category is what sets coal-region research apart. Three specific digitized volumes appear on FamilySearch for the target period (out of 23 total results returned; additional volumes may cover Carbon, Northumberland, and other counties):

| Volume | Coverage | Images | Digitization level |
|---|---|---|---|
| IGN 002240015 | Schuylkill, Mine Worker Employment Cards, 1870–1900 | 410 | **Indexed + images** (95% indexed; full-text searchable) — search by name on FamilySearch |
| IGN 002240016 | Luzerne, Mine Accident Reports, 1870–1895 | 287 | **Browse-only** — 0% indexed, not full-text; must be browsed image by image |
| IGN 002240017 | Lackawanna, Colliery Payroll Ledgers, 1878–1900 | 199 | **Browse-only** — 0% indexed, not full-text; must be browsed image by image |

Beyond FamilySearch (per FamilySearch Wiki, *Pennsylvania Occupational Records*):
- **Pennsylvania State Mine Inspector Reports** — published annually from 1870; list every fatal accident victim with name, age, nationality, and cause of death. At the **Pennsylvania State Archives**. Critical for any miner who died on the job — often the only death record that exists.
- **Company employment records, payroll ledgers, and accident reports** — survive fragmentarily at the Pennsylvania State Archives and at local historical societies in Schuylkill, Luzerne, and Lackawanna counties. Not comprehensively digitized.
- **UMWA records** (United Mine Workers of America, founded 1890) — membership rolls and grievance files. Cover only the last decade of the target period.

### Probate and court records

- **Probate:** *Pennsylvania, Probate Records, 1683–1994* on FamilySearch — **893,214 records, 446,607 images, indexed** (collection 1417683). Filed at the county Register of Wills from county formation.
- **Court records:** County courts of common pleas held naturalization proceedings (pre-1906), guardianships, civil suits, name changes, and criminal cases that frequently name family members.

### Naturalization records — Critical for immigrant miners

Per the FamilySearch Wiki (*Pennsylvania Occupational Records — Mining Community Sources*):
- Pre-1906, naturalization could be filed in **any court of record** — federal, state, or local. Coal miners frequently naturalized in the county court of common pleas nearest their mine.
- **Schuylkill County naturalization records (1840s–1906)** are at the **Pennsylvania State Archives**.
- Many miners arrived via Philadelphia or New York; early declarations of intention may have been filed at those ports or city courts before the miner relocated to the coalfields. Check both the arrival-city records and the coal-county records.
- **Post-1906:** All naturalizations funneled through federal courts; records at NARA's Philadelphia regional facility.

### Land records

- Pennsylvania is a **state-land state** — original patents derive from the Penn family / Commonwealth Land Office, not the BLM General Land Office. The **Pennsylvania State Land Office (Harrisburg)** holds warrant, survey, and patent records from 1681. (Per FamilySearch Wiki, *Pennsylvania Land Records.*)
- County deed records begin at each county's formation date; held at the county Recorder of Deeds.
- **Tax records:** Assessment lists and tax duplicates survive from the late 18th century in many coal counties and serve as census substitutes — especially valuable to bridge the destroyed 1890 census and for the pre-1850 period.

### Military records

- **Civil War (1861–1865):** Many miners served; Pennsylvania contributed 215+ regiments. Service records and pension files at NARA (Fold3, subscription, online).
- **Spanish-American War (1898):** Service records at NARA.
- Pennsylvania adjutant general records and pension rolls at the **Pennsylvania State Archives**.
- **Fold3** (subscription) and **NARA** are the primary access points.

### Cemetery records

- **FindAGrave** (findagrave.com, free) has extensive coverage of Pennsylvania coal-region cemeteries.
- Mining towns had company-owned, church-affiliated, or ethnically segregated cemeteries; Catholic, Protestant, and ethnic denomination cemeteries each maintained separate burial registers, usually held by the individual parish.
- Sexton records and burial registers held by individual churches or cemetery associations — not systematically digitized.

### Newspaper records

- Coal region papers such as the *Miners' Journal* (Pottsville, est. 1825), the *Scranton Tribune*, and various ethnic-language papers carried obituaries, marriage notices, accident reports, and legal notices.
- **Newspapers.com** (subscription) offers access to many historical Pennsylvania newspapers.
- Pennsylvania State Library and local historical societies hold microfilm runs.
- **Ethnic-language newspapers** (Polish, Lithuanian, Slovak, Welsh, Irish) served immigrant communities and carried vital notices not found in English-language papers. These are largely **not digitized** — consult the Pennsylvania State Library in Harrisburg or local historical societies for microfilm.

### Immigration records

- **Primary ports of entry:** Philadelphia and New York (Castle Garden pre-1892; Ellis Island 1892 onward).
- **Passenger lists:** Indexed on Ancestry.com and FamilySearch. Philadelphia manifests at NARA's Philadelphia facility.
- **Full manifest note:** Passenger lists (arrival manifests) record every person aboard, including infants and young children traveling with parents. When researching a family, examine the full manifest page — all family members will be listed together.

---

## Repository guide

### Online repositories

| Repository | Collections for this place | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch** | Church & Town Records 1708–1985 (1.2M records); County Marriages 1885–1950 (524K); Probate Records 1683–1994 (893K); Death Certificates 1906–1968 (4.2M); Civil Marriages 1677–1950 (312K); Schuylkill Mine Worker Employment Cards 1870–1900 (indexed); Luzerne Mine Accident Reports 1870–1895 (browse-only); Lackawanna Colliery Payroll Ledgers 1878–1900 (browse-only); 20+ additional volumes | Free |
| **Ancestry.com** | U.S. Federal Census (all available years); passenger lists; military records | Subscription |
| **FindAGrave** | Cemetery records for the coal region | Free |
| **Newspapers.com** | Historical newspapers including *Miners' Journal* | Subscription |
| **Fold3** | Military service records and pension files | Subscription |
| **NARA (archives.gov)** | Federal naturalization records (post-1906); military records | Free / in-person |

### Physical repositories

| Repository | Holdings | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **Pennsylvania State Archives (Harrisburg)** | Mine Inspector Reports (1870+); Schuylkill County naturalization records; Land Office records; state military records; many county-level records | In-person; some online finding aids |
| **Anthracite Heritage Museum (Scranton)** | Local mining community records, photographs, employment records | In-person |
| **Historical Society of Schuylkill County (Pottsville)** | Local history, church records, newspapers | In-person |
| **County courthouses** (Register of Wills, Recorder of Deeds, Prothonotary) | Probate, deeds, court records, naturalization | In-person / mail |
| **Diocese of Scranton archives** | Catholic parish registers for Lackawanna and surrounding area | Contact by mail/email |
| **FamilySearch Family History Library (Salt Lake City)** | Extensive microfilm of PA county records; accessible at local FamilySearch centers | In-person or via FamilySearch center |

---

## Records NOT online

- Most **ethnic-language church registers** (Polish, Lithuanian, Slovak, Ukrainian, Welsh) — at individual parishes or diocesan archives; not systematically digitized
- **UMWA membership rolls and grievance files** — physical, held by UMWA archives
- Bulk of **company payroll and employment records** beyond the three named FamilySearch volumes — fragmentarily at PA State Archives and local historical societies
- **Ethnic-language newspapers** — largely microfilm at PA State Library or local historical societies; not digitized
- **County naturalization records** beyond Schuylkill — many on FHL microfilm but not all indexed; some physical at county courthouses

---

## Known record losses

- **1890 federal census:** Largely destroyed by fire — a significant gap for the peak immigration period. Substitute with tax records, city directories, state/local enumerations, and (from 1890 onward) UMWA records.
- **Pre-1906 birth and death records for the coal counties:** These were never created — not lost. Civil registration simply did not exist for most of the target period. Church records are the only substitute for this entire era.
- No major courthouse fires in the primary anthracite coal counties are documented in the tool results; always verify with individual county historical societies before concluding records are lost.

---

## Research tips

1. **Start with church records, not vital records.** For any birth or death before 1906, civil registration did not exist in the coal counties. The ethnic parish register is your primary source.

2. **Identify the ethnic community first.** Irish Catholic, Welsh Congregational, Polish Catholic, Lithuanian Catholic, and Slovak Catholic communities each had their own parishes, newspapers, and mutual aid societies. Knowing a miner's nationality narrows you to the right church and records.

3. **Use the Mine Inspector Reports.** Published annually from 1870 and held at the PA State Archives, these name every fatal accident victim with age, nationality, and cause of death. If an ancestor died in the mines, this may be the only death record that exists.

4. **Lackawanna County boundary rule:** Any event in the Scranton/Carbondale area before 1878 was recorded under **Luzerne County**. Search Luzerne County records first for pre-1878 events; treat it as one continuous research trail that passes through a name change, not two separate counties.

5. **Search for naturalization near the mines.** Pre-1906 naturalizations were filed in whichever court of record was convenient. Coal miners typically naturalized in the county court of common pleas where the mine was located — not necessarily where they first arrived.

6. **The Schuylkill Mine Worker Employment Cards** (IGN 002240015) are 95% name-indexed and full-text searchable on FamilySearch — search directly by name. The Luzerne Mine Accident Reports (IGN 002240016) and Lackawanna Colliery Payroll Ledgers (IGN 002240017) must be browsed image by image — budget time for this.

7. **Chain migration.** Immigrant miners commonly recruited family members and neighbors from the same home village. Researching associates in the same mine or boarding house may reveal siblings or cousins sharing the same origin.

8. **Passenger lists list the whole family.** Arrival manifests record every person aboard including infants. When tracing an immigrant miner, pull the full manifest page to find spouse, children, and traveling relatives listed alongside him.

9. **Check the Anthracite Heritage Museum (Scranton) and the Historical Society of Schuylkill County (Pottsville)** for local mining collections not available online.

10. **For post-1906 deaths of parents born in the 1840s–1870s**, the Pennsylvania Death Certificates collection on FamilySearch (4.2M indexed records with images) is fully searchable and will often supply the missing vital data along with parents' names and birthplaces.

I'll survey Massachusetts records for the early Puritan settlement period. Let me start by establishing the jurisdictional context.Good — `standardPlace` is **Massachusetts, United States**. Now I'll fire all survey calls in a single parallel batch.Good data returned. Now let me read the Massachusetts Genealogy wiki article for deeper detail.Here is your locality guide for early colonial Massachusetts:

---

# 📜 Massachusetts Early Colonial Locality Guide
**Place:** Massachusetts, United States
**Period:** c. 1620–1700 (Puritan Settlement Era)

---

## Jurisdictional Context

This period spans **two separate colonial jurisdictions** that merged in 1691 — a critical distinction that determines which record set you search:

- **Plymouth Colony (1620–1691):** Founded by the Separatist Pilgrims. Governed entirely independently. Its records are published and indexed as a distinct set. Towns include Plymouth, Duxbury, Marshfield, Barnstable, Sandwich, and Taunton.
- **Massachusetts Bay Colony (1628–1691):** The Puritan colony dominating the Great Migration (c. 1630–1643), which brought approximately 20,000 settlers. Governed from Boston.
- **Province of Massachusetts Bay (1692–):** Formed when the 1691 royal charter merged both colonies. Records unify from this date forward.

County government was established in the **1640s–1650s** (Suffolk, Essex, Middlesex, Norfolk). Land and probate records were created at the county level from the 1630s; towns predated counties.

> ⚠️ **Critical quirk:** Plymouth Colony records are published and indexed **separately** from Massachusetts Bay Colony records. Determine which colony your ancestor lived in before you search.

---

## Record Types, Collections & Repositories

### 1. 🗒️ Town Vital Records (Birth, Marriage, Death)
**Dates:** 1639 onward (mandatory civil recording)
**Significance:** One of the earliest civil vital registration systems in North America — Massachusetts Bay Colony required towns to record vital events beginning **1639**.

**FamilySearch (indexed):**
- **Massachusetts, Town Vital Records, 1620–1890** — Collection [2061550](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/2061550) — 3,456,789 indexed records / 1,728,394 images. ✅ **Name-searchable.**

**Published abstracts:** The *Vital Records of [Town Name] to 1850* series (Tanner–Corbin series) abstracts town clerk records for **200+ Massachusetts towns** and is often more complete for 17th-century entries than online indexes — consult it even after searching FamilySearch.

**Physical:** Original records remain at individual **town halls**. The **Massachusetts Archives** (Boston) holds early colony-era originals.

---

### 2. ⛪ Congregational Church Records
**Dates:** From 1630
**Significance:** As the established church of the colony, Congregational meetings recorded **members, baptisms, marriages, and disciplinary actions** (excommunications, admonishments). These are often the **only source** for vital events before 1639 and for families in towns with poor civil records.

**FamilySearch (indexed):**
- **Massachusetts, Church Records, 1630–1920** — Collection [1921657](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1921657) — 987,654 indexed records / 493,827 images. ✅ **Name-searchable.**

**Physical/Published:** Many early church records are published in the *Colonial Society of Massachusetts* series and local historical society volumes. **NEHGS** holds manuscript collections of unpublished registers. Individual church archives (some of the oldest Congregational churches in the U.S.) may hold records not yet digitized.

---

### 3. 📃 Probate Records
**Dates:** From 1635
**Significance:** County probate courts recorded wills, inventories, and administrations. These survive in remarkably complete runs and frequently name **children, spouses, and neighbors** — invaluable for reconstructing families.

**FamilySearch (indexed):**
- **Massachusetts, Probate Records, 1635–1991** — Collection [1678901](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1678901) — 567,890 indexed records / 283,945 images. ✅ **Name-searchable.**

**Physical:** Originals at county probate courts (Suffolk, Essex, Middlesex, Norfolk, etc.). Published abstracts exist for some early counties.

---

### 4. 📋 Land Records (Deeds)
**Dates:** From 1630s
**Significance:** County-level deed books survive in remarkably complete runs. They name grantors, grantees, witnesses, and sometimes familial relationships.

**Digitization status:** ⚠️ No name-indexed FamilySearch collection for early Massachusetts deeds (pre-1700) appeared in this survey. Deeds for this period are likely **browse-only or physical-only**. Check the **FamilySearch Catalog** by individual county for available microfilmed volumes to browse.

**Physical:** **County Registries of Deeds** (e.g., Suffolk Registry of Deeds, Essex County Registry of Deeds) hold originals from the 1630s. The Massachusetts Archives holds early colony land grants.

---

### 5. ⚖️ Court Records
**Dates:** From 1620s
**Significance:** Court of Assistants, county courts, and quarter sessions recorded civil cases, criminal proceedings, guardianships, and inquisitions. These records frequently name **family members and neighbors** and serve as vital-record substitutes.

**Digitization status:** ⚠️ Not returned as an indexed FamilySearch collection for this period — likely **physical or microfilm only**. The **Massachusetts Archives** holds original General Court and Court of Assistants records from the 1620s onward.

**Published:** Extensively transcribed and published — e.g., *Records of the Court of Assistants of the Colony of Massachusetts Bay* and the *Essex County Court Records* series.

---

### 6. 🚢 Plymouth Colony Records (1620–1691)
**Dates:** 1620–1691 (entirely separate jurisdiction)
**Significance:** Plymouth Colony operated independently until the 1691 charter merger. Records cover Plymouth-territory towns for the full early colonial period.

**Published (primary access route):**
- *Records of the Colony of New Plymouth in New England* (Shurtleff & Pulsifer, 12 volumes)
- *Mayflower Families Through Five Generations* (General Society of Mayflower Descendants)
- *The Mayflower Descendant* (NEHGS/GSM periodical — indexed back to vol. 1)

**FamilySearch:** Check the FamilySearch Catalog under "Plymouth Colony" and individual Plymouth-area towns for available microfilm/digital volumes.

---

### 7. 🌊 Great Migration Settlers & Passenger Records (1620–1643)
**Dates:** c. 1620–1643
**Significance:** The Great Migration brought ~20,000 settlers to Massachusetts. **Formal passenger manifests for this era are rare** — most ships did not file passenger lists. The research tool of record is the *Great Migration* study series.

**Key resource:** The **Great Migration Study Project** (NEHGS), edited by Robert Charles Anderson, documents every known settler arriving 1620–1643 with evidence-based family sketches. Available at **americanancestors.org** (subscription).

> ❌ **Note:** The FamilySearch collection "Massachusetts, Passenger Lists, 1820–1891" is entirely outside the 1620–1700 target period — do not use it for this era. NARA holds no 17th-century Massachusetts passenger manifests. The *Great Migration* series and **English parish records** are the correct substitutes.

---

### 8. ⚔️ Militia & Military Records
**Dates:** From 1630s; King Philip's War (1675–1676)
**Significance:** Colonial militia musters, training rolls, and expedition records — especially from King Philip's War — document many male colonists.

**Digitization status:** ⚠️ Likely **physical/microfilm only** for this period. The **Massachusetts Archives** and **Fold3** (fold3.com) hold some colonial militia records.

---

## Key Repositories at a Glance

| Repository | Holdings for This Period | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch** (familysearch.org) | Town vitals, church records, probate (see above) | Free online |
| **Massachusetts Archives** (Boston) | Colony records, court records, governor's correspondence from 1620s | In-person; some digitized |
| **NEHGS — americanancestors.org** | *Great Migration* series; manuscript collections; Tanner–Corbin vitals; largest New England genealogy library | Subscription + Boston reading room |
| **County Registries of Deeds** (Suffolk, Essex, Middlesex, Norfolk, etc.) | Deed books from 1630s | In-person; some microfilmed via FamilySearch Catalog |
| **County Probate Courts** | Probate files from 1635 | In-person; partially indexed on FamilySearch |
| **Pilgrim Society / Mayflower Society** (Plymouth, MA) | Plymouth Colony research; Mayflower lineage | pilgrimhall.org; GSM membership |

---

## Research Tips & Important Quirks

1. **Work both colonies.** Plymouth Colony (1620–1691) and Massachusetts Bay Colony (1628–1691) have separate record systems. Establish which colony your town belonged to before searching.
2. **The 1639 mandate is your baseline.** Town vital recording became mandatory in 1639. For arrivals *before* 1639, church records and court records are the primary substitutes.
3. **Pre-1692 county boundaries matter.** Know which county your ancestor's town belonged to at the relevant date — deeds and probate are county-level records filed at the county seat.
4. **The *Great Migration* series is essential for immigrant origins.** For any settler arriving 1620–1643, Robert Charles Anderson's volumes are the most authoritative compiled source and can point you toward English origins.
5. **Published Tanner–Corbin town vitals often beat online indexes.** These published abstracts (200+ towns) frequently capture 17th-century entries that online FamilySearch indexes miss — use them as a parallel check.
6. **No federal census before 1790.** Town tax lists, militia musters, and county court records are the census substitutes for 1620–1700.
7. **English parish records are often essential.** Many Great Migration immigrants can be traced back to specific English parishes via FamilySearch or county record offices in England. English origins are frequently findable for documented settlers.
8. **King Philip's War (1675–1676) disrupted frontier towns.** Some western and southeastern towns were destroyed; check whether your ancestor's town was a conflict zone, as records there may be incomplete or lost.

---

## Digitization Summary

| Record Type | FamilySearch Status | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Town Vital Records 1620–1890 | ✅ Indexed + images | Collection 2061550; 3.4M records |
| Church Records 1630–1920 | ✅ Indexed + images | Collection 1921657; ~988K records |
| Probate Records 1635–1991 | ✅ Indexed + images | Collection 1678901; ~568K records |
| Land/Deed Records (pre-1700) | ⚠️ Physical/browse-only | Check FamilySearch Catalog by county |
| Court Records (pre-1700) | ⚠️ Physical/microfilm | Massachusetts Archives; published series |
| Plymouth Colony Records | ⚠️ Partially digitized | Extensively published; check FS Catalog |
| Passenger Lists (pre-1700) | ❌ None for this era | Use *Great Migration* series instead |
| Militia/Military (colonial) | ⚠️ Physical/microfilm | Massachusetts Archives; Fold3 |

---

**Where to start:** If your ancestor arrived in Massachusetts Bay Colony 1630–1643, begin with the **Great Migration Study Project** at americanancestors.org, then cross into **FamilySearch Collection 2061550** (town vitals) for the specific town. If the family is in Plymouth Colony territory, start with the published *Records of the Colony of New Plymouth* and the Mayflower Society resources. Probate and church records fill in family structure throughout the century.

Now let me load the remaining MCP tools and run the full parallel survey:Now let me read the specific Oklahoma wiki pages from the search results:I now have enough from the tools to compile the full guide. Here is the locality research guide:

---

# 🗺️ Locality Research Guide
## Oklahoma — Land Run of 1889 / Territorial Era (1889–1907)

---

## Jurisdictional Context

**This is a federal-land state with a uniquely compressed jurisdictional history** — everything below is shaped by it:

| Period | Jurisdiction | Significance |
|--------|-------------|--------------|
| Before Apr. 22, 1889 | "Unassigned Lands" within Indian Territory | No permanent settler government; no county records |
| Apr. 22 – May 1, 1890 | Post-Run limbo | Homestead claims filed but no territorial government yet |
| May 2, 1890 – Nov. 16, 1907 | **Oklahoma Territory** (Organic Act) | Counties formed; territorial courts, county records begin |
| Nov. 16, 1907 onward | **State of Oklahoma** (46th state) | Oklahoma Territory + Indian Territory combined |

FamilySearch's standard place `Oklahoma, United States` carries a date range of 1907–present (statehood). **Records from 1889–1907 were created under Oklahoma Territory**, held at the National Archives at Fort Worth and the Oklahoma State Archives — not state repositories. Always search both the territorial-era collections and the later state-level collections as one continuous trail.

---

## Record-by-Record Survey

### 🏆 1. Land Records *(the premier source for this period)*

The Land Run generated a uniquely rich federal paper trail. Every settler had to file a homestead application and later prove up — meaning multiple documents per family.

**FamilySearch collection — indexed + images:**
- **"Oklahoma and Indian Territory, Early Land Records, 1834–1907"** — 234,567 records / 117,283 images
  [https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1541872](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1541872)
  Covers homestead applications and early grants.

**Bureau of Land Management — General Land Office Records** (`glorecords.blm.gov`)
Freely searchable database of land patents. Homestead files typically contain: original application, proof-of-settlement affidavit (with witnesses who can become new research leads), and the final patent. **Rejected applications also survive** and often contain family details. [Source: FamilySearch Wiki, "Oklahoma Land Records — Land Run and Homestead Records"]

**National Archives, Washington D.C. — Record Group 49 (General Land Office)**
Full homestead case files, partially indexed on FamilySearch and BLM GLO. The two original land offices opened April 22, 1889 were located at **Guthrie** and **Kingfisher** — knowing which land office served your ancestor's claim narrows the file search.

> ⚠️ **Quirk:** Records from the April–May 1889 period (between the Run and territorial organization) are sparse — no county government existed yet. The first county-level records appear after May 1890.

---

### 📊 2. Census Records

| Census | Status | Notes |
|--------|--------|-------|
| **1880 Federal** | Fully indexed | Ancestors almost certainly enumerated in other states — search **departure states** (Kansas, Missouri, Texas, Arkansas) |
| **1890 Federal** | ❌ Largely destroyed (1921 fire) | See substitute below |
| **1890 Oklahoma Territory Special Census** | ✅ **Survives** at Oklahoma Historical Society | One of the very few surviving 1890 census records in the nation; held at `okhistory.org` |
| **1900 Federal** | Fully indexed | First complete federal census for Oklahoma Territory residents — name-indexed on FamilySearch and Ancestry |
| **Indian Censuses & Rolls, 1851–1959** | Indexed + images on FamilySearch | See below |

**FamilySearch collection:**
- **"Oklahoma, Indian Censuses and Rolls, 1851–1959"** — 567,890 records / 283,945 images
  [https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1987432](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1987432)
  Essential if your family had Native American ancestry or intermarried with tribal members.

> ⚠️ **Quirk:** The Dawes Rolls (1898–1914) enrolled members of the Five Civilized Tribes and are critical even for non-Native families who registered as intermarried citizens.

---

### 💍 3. Vital Records

**No statewide civil registration existed until 1908** (after statehood). For the 1889–1907 period:

- **Marriages:** County clerks began recording marriages when counties were organized in 1890.
  - **FamilySearch: "Oklahoma, County Marriages, 1890–1995"** — 456,789 records / 228,394 images (indexed + images)
    [https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/2134789](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/2134789)
- **Births & Deaths (1889–1907):** No systematic civil registration — **church records are the primary alternative**. Newspapers also published birth and death notices almost from day one.

---

### ⛪ 4. Church Records

Denominations (Methodist, Baptist, Presbyterian) established congregations in the new towns almost immediately after April 22, 1889. Church records are the main substitute for absent birth and death registration in this period. [Source: FamilySearch Wiki, "Oklahoma Territorial Records — Territorial Courts and Early Settlement"]

**FamilySearch collection — indexed + images:**
- **"Oklahoma, Mission and Church Records, 1820–1965"** — 123,456 records / 61,728 images
  [https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1654321](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1654321)
  Includes pre-Run mission records from Indian Territory as well as territorial-era congregation registers.

---

### ⚖️ 5. Territorial Court Records *(1890–1907)*

[Source: FamilySearch Wiki, "Oklahoma Territorial Records — Territorial Courts and Early Settlement"]

- **National Archives at Fort Worth** — the regional NARA facility for Oklahoma. Holds district court civil and criminal cases, **probate records**, and **naturalization records** for the territorial period.
- **Oklahoma State Archives** (Oklahoma City) — additional territorial records.
- Naturalization records: Pre-1906 naturalizations were filed in *any* court of record; search both federal district courts and territorial district courts.
- County-level tax rolls from the 1890s also survive and help document early settlers.

---

### 📰 6. Newspapers

Oklahoma newspapers sprang up within days of the Land Run. **Guthrie** and **Oklahoma City** both had papers running by late April 1889.

- **Newspapers.com** — searchable historical Oklahoma newspapers from 1889 onward (subscription)
- **Oklahoma Historical Society** (`okhistory.org`) — holds an extensive collection of territorial newspapers, including many not yet on commercial platforms

Newspapers are especially valuable for: arrival announcements, claim-filing notices, land dispute coverage, early death notices, and community social columns.

---

### 🪖 7. Military Records

Many Land Run participants were Civil War veterans or their sons.

- **Fold3** (`fold3.com`) — Civil War service records and pension files; pension files often contain extensive family details, including birthplaces and family members' names
- **NARA Washington, D.C.** — original service and pension records

---

### 🏛️ Key Repositories at a Glance

| Repository | What It Holds | Access |
|------------|--------------|--------|
| **BLM GLO Records** (`glorecords.blm.gov`) | Land patents, homestead certificates | Free online |
| **National Archives, Washington D.C. (RG 49)** | Full homestead case files | In-person / NARA catalog online |
| **National Archives at Fort Worth** | Territorial court, probate, naturalization records | In-person / some digitized on FamilySearch |
| **Oklahoma Historical Society** (`okhistory.org`) | 1890 special census, territorial newspapers, photos | In-person + some online |
| **Oklahoma State Archives** (Oklahoma City) | Territorial and early state records | In-person |
| **FamilySearch** (`familysearch.org`) | Land records, Indian rolls, county marriages, church records | Free online |
| **Ancestry.com** | U.S. federal censuses, Dawes Rolls, newspapers | Subscription |
| **Fold3** | Military pension files | Subscription |
| **Newspapers.com** | Territorial-era Oklahoma newspapers | Subscription |
| **FindAGrave** (`findagrave.com`) | Cemetery records | Free online |

---

## ⚠️ Critical Research Quirks

1. **Search departure-state records first.** Land Run settlers predominantly came from Kansas, Missouri, Texas, and Arkansas. Their birth, marriage, and pre-1889 census records are in those states — not Oklahoma. Establish the origin state before focusing on Oklahoma records.

2. **The 1890 federal census for Oklahoma is destroyed** — use the Oklahoma Historical Society's 1890 Territory special census as the substitute.

3. **No vital registration before 1908** — church records and newspapers are the primary birth/death sources for 1889–1907.

4. **"Sooners"** (those who entered early) were sometimes disqualified from claims and may appear in contest/dispute records at the land offices — a useful, often-overlooked source.

5. **Records 1889–1907 are territorial, not state records.** They are held at the National Archives at Fort Worth and the Oklahoma State Archives, not at a state vital records office.

6. **Indian Territory and Oklahoma Territory were legally distinct** until 1907. Families in the Indian Territory (eastern Oklahoma) have a different records trail — Five Civilized Tribes courts, Dawes Commission enrollment files — from those in Oklahoma Territory (western Oklahoma).

7. **FamilySearch wiki pages for Oklahoma** returned generic content in this survey rather than Oklahoma-specific guidance; consult the wiki directly at [familysearch.org/en/wiki/Oklahoma](https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Oklahoma) for the most current article.

---

## Recommended Starting Sequence

1. **BLM GLO** — confirm the land patent and claim location
2. **FamilySearch "Oklahoma and Indian Territory, Early Land Records"** — homestead application details
3. **1900 Federal Census** (FamilySearch / Ancestry) — establishes the family in place
4. **1890 Oklahoma Territory Special Census** (Oklahoma Historical Society) — bridges the gap
5. **FamilySearch "Oklahoma, County Marriages"** — if seeking a marriage 1890–1907
6. **National Archives at Fort Worth** — probate and naturalization if needed
7. **Departure-state records** (Kansas, Missouri, Texas, etc.) — for pre-1889 family history
8. **Newspapers.com / OHS newspaper collection** — community context and vital event notices

---

# Locality Guide: Alabama (Pre-Statehood, c. 1780–1819)

---

## Jurisdiction overview

The single most important fact for circa-1800 Alabama research: **"Alabama" did not exist as a jurisdiction in 1800.** The region was split across multiple, very different authorities:

| Period | Authority over present-day Alabama |
|--------|-------------------------------------|
| Before 1702 | Native American territory (Creek, Cherokee) — no European records |
| 1702–1763 | French colony (Province of Louisiana) — Mobile founded 1702 |
| 1763–1780 | British colony (Province of West Florida) |
| 1780–1813 | Spanish colony — Mobile District (Spain seized Mobile 1780) |
| 1798–1817 | U.S. Mississippi Territory — covers present-day AL and MS; **Mobile remained Spanish until 1813** |
| 1813–1817 | Mobile District incorporated into Mississippi Territory |
| 1817–1819 | Alabama Territory (eastern half of former Mississippi Territory) |
| Dec 14, 1819 | **Alabama achieves statehood** |

Around 1800, the only established European settlement was **Mobile** (under Spain). The rest of present-day Alabama was either sparsely settled American frontier under the Mississippi Territory or unceded Creek and Cherokee land. The first U.S. counties organized in present-day Alabama were Washington County (1800) and Madison County (1808); most others formed 1818–1819.

- **Population:** Extremely sparse. The American settler population in the Alabama districts of the Mississippi Territory was roughly 4,000–9,000 between 1800 and 1810, concentrated in Washington County in the south. The great wave of settlement came *after* the Creek War of 1813–1814.
- **Economy:** Subsistence farming, trade with Native nations, Spanish colonial commerce at Mobile.
- **Religion:** Catholic at Mobile (under Spanish administration); scattered Methodist and Baptist circuit riders began appearing in the north only ca. 1808–1810. No Protestant church infrastructure in 1800.
- **Key events:** Creek War 1813–1814 (opens most of central/northern Alabama to American settlement); War of 1812.

---

## Boundary changes

Records follow the creating jurisdiction, not modern boundaries. For pre-statehood Alabama research, this means working *three* distinct jurisdictional tracks simultaneously:

1. **Mississippi Territory track (American settler records, 1798–1817):** Any American settler in present-day Alabama ca. 1800 was legally resident in the Mississippi Territory, headquartered at Natchez. Washington County (organized 1800 from the older Tombigbee District) was the *only* organized American county in present-day Alabama until 1808. Every American settler — regardless of where in present-day Alabama they lived — fell under Washington County's jurisdiction for the period 1800–1808. Search Washington County records and Mississippi Territory records before any later Alabama county records.

2. **Alabama Territory track (1817–1819):** After the territory was separated from Mississippi in 1817, records began to be generated under Alabama's own territorial government. County formation accelerated: Lawrence, Limestone, Cahaba, Shelby, and others formed 1818–1819.

3. **Spanish colonial track (Mobile area, pre-1813):** Mobile and its hinterland were a Spanish colonial city. Records are in Spanish (sometimes French), held in European archives, and require a completely different research strategy from the American territorial records.

---

## Available record types

### Vital records (civil registration)
- **Start date:** Alabama did not begin *statewide* civil registration until **1908** (births and deaths). Marriage records begin at county organization.
- **Earliest Alabama marriages on FamilySearch:** Collection **"Alabama, County Marriages, 1809–1950"** (ID 1598408; 612,345 records, 306,172 images) — **indexed + images**. The 1809 start date reflects the earliest organized counties; no marriage records from this collection cover 1800 itself.
- **For the Mobile area before 1813:** Catholic sacramental registers (baptism, marriage, burial) maintained by the Church of the Immaculate Conception are the only surviving vital-event records. These are not in mainstream US genealogy databases; see Church records below.
- **No civil registration exists anywhere in present-day Alabama for circa 1800.** Church records, land entries, tax lists, and census substitutes are the primary sources.

### Church records
- **Catholic (Mobile area):** The Church of the Immaculate Conception, Mobile, maintained sacramental registers under French and Spanish administration from the early 1700s. These are the richest vital-event records available for any Alabama location around 1800. **Language:** French and Spanish through the colonial period. **Repositories:** Mobile Municipal Archives (microfilm); Catholic Diocese of Mobile Archives; University of Notre Dame microfilm collections; some held at the Library of Congress and ADAH. Partial microfilm by FamilySearch.
- **Protestant (northern/central Alabama):** Methodist and Baptist circuit riders began ca. 1808–1810; congregation records from this era are scattered and survival is poor. Virtually no organized Protestant church infrastructure existed in present-day Alabama in 1800.
- **FamilySearch collection:** **"Alabama, Church Records, 1803–1950"** (ID 1986396; 156,789 records, 78,394 images) — **indexed + images**. Earliest date is 1803. Coverage within the collection for the 1803–1810 window is likely thin given how few congregations existed.

### Census records
- **Federal census:** The **1800 federal census** covered the Mississippi Territory, which included present-day Alabama. The **1810 census** likewise covered the Territory. Alabama first appears as its own entity in the **1820 census** (first census as a state). The 1800 and 1810 schedules for the Alabama districts are held at NARA and are available on Ancestry.
- **Territorial censuses:** The Mississippi Territory conducted separate enumerations in **1801, 1805, 1808, and 1810** covering settlers in present-day Alabama. According to the FamilySearch Wiki's Alabama Genealogy article, these are held at the **Alabama Department of Archives and History (ADAH)** in Montgomery. *The FamilySearch volume search returned zero results* for this period — these enumerations do not appear to be digitized and browsable on FamilySearch. Check ADAH directly (archives.alabama.gov).
- **FamilySearch collection:** **"Alabama, Compiled Census and Census Substitutes Index, 1810–1890"** (ID 2016756; 287,634 records, **zero images**) — **index only, no images.** The earliest date is 1810; it includes both census data and census substitutes (tax lists, etc.) but provides no scan access.
- **Mississippi side:** **"Mississippi, Compiled Census and Census Substitutes, 1792–1890"** (ID 1927171; 198,340 records, **zero images**) — **index only.** The 1792 start may include early enumerations relevant to the shared territorial period; worth searching for families near the eventual state boundary.
- **Spanish colonial census:** Spain conducted *padrónes* (census returns) of the Mobile District. Held at the Archivo General de Indias, Seville; copies at the Library of Congress.
- **⚠️ Digitization gap:** No digitized, browsable volume covering Alabama-area territorial census or census substitutes from 1800–1810 was returned by the FamilySearch volume search. These records exist at ADAH but are not confirmed as accessible online.

### Land records
**This is the single richest record category for pre-statehood Alabama research.**

- **System:** Alabama is a **federal land state** — original land title flowed from the U.S. government. No state-issued land grants exist for the public-land period.
- **BLM General Land Office (glorecords.blm.gov):** Federal land patents for Alabama, including territorial-era grants beginning **ca. 1803** (when the St. Stephens Land Office opened), are fully indexed and imaged here. **Free access.** The Huntsville Land Office opened in 1811. A land entry places your ancestor at a specific location at a specific time — this is often the only surviving dated record for pre-1820 Alabama settlers.
- **Spanish land grants (Mobile area):** Spain issued land grants in the Mobile District. After the U.S. took Mobile in 1813, Congress created a Board of Land Commissioners to adjudicate Spanish (and French/British) land claims. These claim files are at NARA (RG 49) and ADAH, and are a unique source for Mobile-area families.
- **FamilySearch (Mississippi side):** **"Mississippi, Territorial and State Land Records, 1798–1907"** (ID 1919694; 312,450 records, 156,225 images) — **indexed + images** — covers the Mississippi half of the former territory from 1798. Land entries near the Mississippi-Alabama border may be filed here; worth searching for families near that boundary.
- **County deed books:** Washington County (from ca. 1800) holds the earliest deed records for any American-administered location in present-day Alabama. Originals at the Washington County Courthouse, Chatom, AL; some microfilmed by ADAH.

### Probate and court records
- **Alabama probate on FamilySearch:** **"Alabama, Probate Records, 1809–1985"** (ID 1537764; 438,210 records, 219,105 images) — **indexed + images.** Starts 1809, aligned with the first generation of organized counties beyond Washington.
- **Washington County (ca. 1800):** The courthouse in Chatom holds the earliest probate and court records for American-administered Alabama, beginning ca. 1800. Some have been microfilmed by ADAH.
- **Mississippi Territory courts:** The territorial government at Natchez exercised jurisdiction over all of present-day Alabama. **"Mississippi, County Court Records, 1799–1920"** (ID 1855293; 245,670 records, 122,835 images) — **indexed + images** — covers the Mississippi side. Equivalent Alabama-side territorial court records are less clearly represented in online databases; check ADAH.
- **Spanish colonial courts (Mobile):** Estate proceedings, land disputes, and civil cases under Spain are at the Archivo General de Indias and (in transcription/copy) at ADAH and the Library of Congress.

### Military records
- **Creek War (1813–1814):** Pension applications filed by Creek War veterans are among the most biographical records available for early Alabama settlers. They often record birth date, birthplace, marriage, and family members. Held at NARA (RG 15); indexed and imaged on Fold3 and partially on FamilySearch.
- **War of 1812:** Alabama Territory settlers served. Pension and service records at NARA; Fold3 (fold3.com) has a significant digitized collection. FamilySearch's curated external links for Alabama include Fold3.
- **Mississippi Territory militia:** Muster rolls and militia lists survive in scattered form at ADAH and NARA. Not confirmed as consistently indexed online.

### Cemetery records
- **Coverage is sparse.** Most pre-1820 burials were in church burial grounds (Catholic at Mobile; small Protestant plots in the north) or on private land. Formal sexton records did not exist.
- **FindAGrave.com** covers some early Alabama cemeteries but pre-1820 headstones are few and often damaged or missing.
- **Mobile Catholic burials:** The Church of the Immaculate Conception's burial registers are the most complete record of deaths in early Mobile; see Church records above.

### Newspaper records
- **No newspapers in present-day Alabama before ca. 1811.** Mobile's first paper (*The Mobile Centinel*) began in 1811; northern Alabama's first newspapers appeared ca. 1816–1818.
- Early issues are held at ADAH and the University of Alabama Libraries; limited digitization online.
- **Not useful for research centered on 1800 itself.**

### Tax records
- **Washington County tax lists (from ca. 1800):** Among the most important substitutes for the missing census and vital records of this period. Typically record the head of household plus taxable property (land, livestock, enslaved persons). Held at ADAH; partially indexed in the "Compiled Census and Census Substitutes" collection (which has no images online).
- **Mississippi Territory tax records:** Exist for the American-administered districts; held at ADAH and NARA.
- **Spanish colonial taxes:** Collected in the Mobile District; records at the Archivo General de Indias and (in copies) the Library of Congress.
- **Research value:** For the period 1800–1810, tax lists are often the *only* surviving annual record of a settler's presence in a specific location.

### Other records
- **Militia / muster rolls:** Mississippi Territory militia rolls survive at ADAH and NARA. Important for identifying male residents of the territory.
- **Spanish colonial records — full scope:** Beyond church registers and census, Spanish records include land grants, administrative correspondence, trade licenses, and judicial records. Primary repository: Archivo General de Indias, Seville. Microfilm copies: Library of Congress; Mobile Municipal Archives.
- **French colonial records (Mobile, 1702–1763):** Held at Archives Nationales d'Outre-Mer, Aix-en-Provence; microfilm at the Mobile Municipal Archives.
- **Creek/Cherokee treaty records:** Land cession documents (1805–1816) and related federal treaty proceedings at NARA may name Creek and Cherokee leaders and signatories for this period.

---

## Repository guide

### Online repositories

| Repository | Collections for this place/period | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch** (familysearch.org) | Alabama Church Records 1803–1950 (1986396); Alabama Probate Records 1809–1985 (1537764); Alabama County Marriages 1809–1950 (1598408); Alabama Compiled Census & Substitutes Index 1810–1890 (2016756, index only); Mississippi Territorial & State Land Records 1798–1907 (1919694); Mississippi County Court Records 1799–1920 (1855293); Mississippi Compiled Census & Substitutes 1792–1890 (1927171, index only); Mississippi Church Records 1800–1950 (2034891) | Free |
| **BLM General Land Office** (glorecords.blm.gov) | Federal land patents for Alabama from ca. 1803 — indexed + images | Free |
| **Ancestry** (ancestry.com) | U.S. Federal Census 1800 (Mississippi Territory), 1810 (Territory), 1820 (Alabama state) | Subscription |
| **Fold3** (fold3.com) | Military service records; Creek War and War of 1812 pension files | Subscription |
| **FindAGrave** (findagrave.com) | Cemetery records (very sparse for pre-1820) | Free |
| **NARA** (archives.gov) | Finding aids; some pension files digitized | Free/subscription |
| **ADAH online** (archives.alabama.gov) | Some digitized territorial-era records; catalog of holdings | Free |

### Physical repositories

| Repository | Holdings | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **Alabama Dept. of Archives and History (ADAH)**, Montgomery, AL | Territorial census enumerations (1801, 1805, 1808, 1810); tax lists; militia rolls; Spanish land claim records; county record microfilm | In-person; some online |
| **Washington County Courthouse**, Chatom, AL | Earliest county-level Alabama records (deeds, probate, court) from ca. 1800 | In-person / mail |
| **NARA Southeast Region**, Morrow, GA | Federal land entry papers; military pension files; territorial court records | In-person / mail |
| **Archivo General de Indias**, Seville, Spain | Spanish colonial records: Mobile District census, church registers, land grants, correspondence | In-person; some microfilm at LOC |
| **Library of Congress**, Washington DC | Copies of French and Spanish colonial records; early territorial documents | In-person; some at loc.gov |
| **Mobile Municipal Archives**, Mobile, AL | French and Spanish colonial records (microfilm); early Catholic parish register microfilm | In-person |
| **Catholic Diocese of Mobile Archives**, Mobile, AL | Sacramental registers of Church of the Immaculate Conception from colonial era | In-person / correspondence |

---

## Records NOT online (as of this survey)

- **Territorial census enumerations (1801, 1805, 1808):** Physically at ADAH. The FamilySearch volume search returned *zero results* for Alabama 1780–1820 — these are not confirmed as digitized or browsable online.
- **Washington County deed and court books (ca. 1800–1808):** Some on FamilySearch microfilm, but no indexed/browsable digital volumes returned by the volume search.
- **Spanish colonial records (Mobile District, 1780–1813):** Primary repositories in Seville and Washington DC; not in mainstream US genealogy databases.
- **French colonial records (Mobile, 1702–1763):** Held in France; microfilm at Mobile Municipal Archives only.
- **Early Catholic parish registers (Mobile):** Partial microfilm at ADAH and the Diocese; not confirmed as fully online.
- **Mississippi Territory militia and muster rolls:** At ADAH and NARA; online indexing status unclear.
- **All four FamilySearch Wiki place-page sections** (home, getting_started, online_records, research_tips) for "Alabama, United States" returned only a generic guide, not Alabama-specific content — the wiki pages for this place do not currently provide jurisdiction-specific guidance.

---

## Known record losses

- **Creek War disruption (1813–1814):** Active military conflict across present-day Alabama disrupted settlement and record creation in the southern districts during the conflict period.
- **Intrinsic scarcity (not destruction):** The period before 1808 is sparse simply because American settlement had barely begun. Record creation was minimal by any clerk, anywhere in the territory.
- **Washington County courthouse losses:** Washington County has experienced some early record losses; check with ADAH for the current state of the earliest volumes.
- **General territorial-era gaps:** Many territorial court and administrative records that *should* exist were never well preserved and are physically incomplete at ADAH.

---

## Research tips

1. **Think "Mississippi Territory," not "Alabama."** For 1800 specifically, Alabama had essentially no county structure and no territorial government of its own. Search Mississippi Territory records — especially FamilySearch collections 1919694 (land) and 1927171 (census substitutes) — before expecting to find anything filed under "Alabama."

2. **Washington County is the gateway.** It is the *only* organized American county in present-day Alabama before 1808. Every American settler in the region between 1800 and 1808 — wherever they eventually settled — fell under Washington County's jurisdiction for deeds, court, and probate. Always search Washington County records first.

3. **The BLM GLO is your best free online resource.** Federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov are the richest name-indexed source for early Alabama settlers, beginning with entries from 1803 onward. A land patent is often the earliest and only dated document placing an ancestor in Alabama.

4. **Mobile is a different research universe.** If your subject was in or near Mobile before 1813, you are researching a Spanish colonial city. Spanish language, European archives, and colonial-era finding strategies apply. The Archivo General de Indias and the Library of Congress are your primary entry points, not FamilySearch or Ancestry.

5. **Tax lists substitute for missing census years.** For 1800–1810, territorial and Washington County tax lists are the primary annual substitute for missing census records. They are partially indexed in FamilySearch collection 2016756 (index only, no images); originals at ADAH.

6. **Creek War pension files are biographically rich.** Veterans — often among the earliest American settlers — filed pension applications that regularly include birth date, birthplace, marriage information, and names of family members. Search via Fold3 and NARA.

7. **FamilySearch Alabama collections don't begin until 1803–1809.** The four indexed FamilySearch collections for Alabama all have their earliest records at 1803–1809 (Church Records, County Marriages, Probate, Census Substitutes). For anything in 1800 itself, the FamilySearch Alabama collections will not help; pivot to the Mississippi Territory and Mississippi state collections.

8. **The Mississippi collections may include Alabama-area records.** Because the Mississippi Territory was administered as a unit from Natchez, some Alabama-area records were generated as part of Mississippi's bureaucracy and remain filed under "Mississippi" in FamilySearch. Search FamilySearch collections 1919694 and 1927171 even if your research focuses on the Alabama side of the old territorial line.
